title: Unit 1: DNA is the foundation for the unity and diversity of living things
searchText: unit 1: dna is the foundation for the unity and diversity of living things

title: Chapter 1.1: How does an understanding of DNA help us investigate living things?
searchText: chapter 1.1: how does an understanding of dna help us investigate living things?

title: 1.1 Concept 1: The variation in living things we see around us is due to DNA — p.10–11
searchText: 1.1 concept 1: the variation in living things we see around us is due to dna — p.10–11

title: KP1: DNA as genetic material
searchText: kp1: dna as genetic material dna, or deoxyribonucleic acid, is a double-stranded nucleic acid that stores genetic information. use dna to explain why organisms inherit traits and show variation.

title: KP2: DNA and variation among living things
searchText: kp2: dna and variation among living things differences in dna help produce differences in appearance, structures, and life processes. connect dna differences to variation among plants, animals, bacteria, protists, and fungi.

title: KP3: Shared traits of life
searchText: kp3: shared traits of life living things share basic characteristics such as being made of cells, using energy, growing, and reproducing. distinguish shared life processes from dna-based differences among organisms.

title: KP4: DNA and organism function
searchText: kp4: dna and organism function most cells contain genetic information that influences an organism’s appearance and life processes. explain how dna affects both visible traits and internal cell functions.

title: KP5: DNA across major groups of organisms
searchText: kp5: dna across major groups of organisms dna-based variation appears across many groups, including plants, animals, bacteria, protists, and fungi. use examples from different organism groups to show that dna is connected to life’s diversity.

title: 1.1 Concept 2: DNA is made of many nucleotides linked together in a specific order — p.12–13
searchText: 1.1 concept 2: dna is made of many nucleotides linked together in a specific order — p.12–13

title: KP1: Nucleic acids
searchText: kp1: nucleic acids cells contain nucleic acids such as dna and rna. identify dna and rna as large molecules built from smaller units.

title: KP2: Nucleotide structure
searchText: kp2: nucleotide structure a nucleotide consists of a phosphate group, a sugar, and a nitrogenous base. break dna structure into its repeating building blocks.

title: KP3: DNA nitrogenous bases
searchText: kp3: dna nitrogenous bases the nitrogenous bases in dna are adenine \((a)\), cytosine \((c)\), guanine \((g)\), and thymine \((t)\). recognize the four dna bases used to store genetic information.

title: KP4: Double helix structure
searchText: kp4: double helix structure dna is made of two nucleotide strands arranged like a twisted ladder, or double helix. describe the sugar-phosphate backbone and base-pair “rungs” of dna.

title: KP5: Complementary base pairing
searchText: kp5: complementary base pairing \(a\) pairs with \(t\), and \(g\) pairs with \(c\). determine the complementary dna strand from a given dna sequence.

title: KP6: DNA sequence and protein instructions
searchText: kp6: dna sequence and protein instructions the order of bases in dna stores instructions that tell cells what proteins to make and how to make them. connect dna base sequence to protein production and cell function.

title: KP7: Proteins and organism function
searchText: kp7: proteins and organism function proteins make up much of cell and tissue structure and help control how cells form and function. explain how dna instructions can affect organism development and body processes.

title: KP8: Genome
searchText: kp8: genome the complete dna sequence in each cell of an organism is called the organism’s genome. connect the human genome to about three billion base pairs distributed across 46 chromosomes.

title: 1.1 Concept 3: DNA exists in chromosomes, which contain thousands of genes — p.14–15
searchText: 1.1 concept 3: dna exists in chromosomes, which contain thousands of genes — p.14–15

title: KP1: DNA, chromatin, and chromosomes
searchText: kp1: dna, chromatin, and chromosomes dna exists as chromatin during most of the cell cycle and condenses into chromosomes during cell division. explain how long dna can fit inside a small nucleus.

title: KP2: Chromosome number in human body cells
searchText: kp2: chromosome number in human body cells human somatic cells have 46 chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs. identify normal human chromosome organization.

title: KP3: Autosomes and sex chromosomes
searchText: kp3: autosomes and sex chromosomes humans have 22 pairs of autosomes and one pair of sex chromosomes, usually \(xx\) or \(xy\). distinguish autosomes from sex chromosomes in a chromosome set.

title: KP4: Homologous chromosomes
searchText: kp4: homologous chromosomes homologous chromosomes have the same sequence of genes but are not identical. compare chromosome pairs by length, centromere location, banding pattern, and gene locations.

title: KP5: Genes and alleles
searchText: kp5: genes and alleles a gene is a section of dna that carries information for a trait; an allele is a different form of the same gene. explain how alleles contribute to differences in traits.

title: KP6: Karyotypes
searchText: kp6: karyotypes a karyotype is a photograph of paired homologous chromosomes arranged by size and type. use a karyotype to examine chromosome number, chromosome pairs, and sex chromosomes.

title: 1.1 Concept 4: The structure of DNA is important to passing on genetic information — p.16
searchText: 1.1 concept 4: the structure of dna is important to passing on genetic information — p.16

title: KP1: DNA replication
searchText: kp1: dna replication replication creates an exact copy of a dna molecule. explain why cells must copy dna before cell division.

title: KP2: Genome copying
searchText: kp2: genome copying a cell replicates its whole genome once during the cell cycle. connect genome copying to accurate inheritance of genetic information.

title: KP3: Template strands and complementary strands
searchText: kp3: template strands and complementary strands each new dna molecule contains one original strand and one new complementary strand. use complementary base pairing to explain accurate dna copying.

title: KP4: DNA to RNA to protein
searchText: kp4: dna to rna to protein genetic information passes from dna to an rna copy, and rna directs the assembly of amino acids into a protein. trace how a gene can lead to production of a specific protein.

title: KP5: Base sequence and protein sequence
searchText: kp5: base sequence and protein sequence the sequence of dna bases determines the rna base sequence, which determines the amino acid sequence of a protein. explain why changes in dna sequence can affect protein production.

title: 1.1 Concept 5: The different genetic make-up of organisms is reflected in the diversity of life — p.17–18
searchText: 1.1 concept 5: the different genetic make-up of organisms is reflected in the diversity of life — p.17–18

title: KP1: Biodiversity
searchText: kp1: biodiversity biodiversity includes genetic diversity, species diversity, and ecosystem diversity. classify examples of biodiversity by level.

title: KP2: Genetic diversity
searchText: kp2: genetic diversity genetic diversity is the variety of inherited traits within a species or population. use inherited trait differences to explain variation among individuals.

title: KP3: Gene pool
searchText: kp3: gene pool a gene pool is the sum of all versions, or alleles, of all genes in a population. compare genetic diversity within a population and within a species.

title: KP4: Species and species diversity
searchText: kp4: species and species diversity a species is a group of organisms that can interbreed in nature and produce fertile offspring. describe species diversity as the variety and abundance of species in an area.

title: KP5: Ecosystem diversity
searchText: kp5: ecosystem diversity ecosystem diversity is the variety of ecosystems in the biosphere. connect biotic and abiotic factors to the variety of ecosystems on earth.

title: KP6: Genetic variation and biodiversity
searchText: kp6: genetic variation and biodiversity variation in genes helps support genetic, species, and ecosystem diversity. explain how genetic differences can contribute to the formation and survival of diverse life forms.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: What Are the Accomplishments and Legacies of the Human Genome Project? — p.19
searchText: optional extension — at issue: what are the accomplishments and legacies of the human genome project? — p.19

title: KP1: Human Genome Project goals
searchText: kp1: human genome project goals the human genome project aimed to determine the complete sequence of the human genome and identify all human genes. connect genome sequencing to understanding human genetic information.

title: KP2: Human genome findings
searchText: kp2: human genome findings the human genome has about \(3 10 9\) base pairs and about 21 000 genes. recognize that only a small portion of human dna directly codes for genes.

title: KP3: Genetic information and ethics
searchText: kp3: genetic information and ethics genome research raises social, legal, and ethical questions about ownership, control, and regulation of genetic information. discuss why genetic data requires careful use and protection.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Seeding the Future: Native and Heritage Varieties — p.20
searchText: optional extension — at issue: seeding the future: native and heritage varieties — p.20

title: KP1: Hybrids and crop traits
searchText: kp1: hybrids and crop traits hybrids are created by crossing two plant lines to enhance selected traits such as size, appearance, or shelf life. compare the benefits and limitations of hybrid crop varieties.

title: KP2: Native and heritage varieties
searchText: kp2: native and heritage varieties native and heritage varieties are naturally pollinated, non-patented plants that produce fertile seeds. explain how local adaptation can increase resistance to pests, disease, and environmental stress.

title: KP3: Seed libraries and seed banks
searchText: kp3: seed libraries and seed banks seed libraries distribute seeds, while seed banks preserve seeds for future use. connect seed preservation to maintaining genetic diversity and biodiversity.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 1-A: Using Karyotypes to Diagnose Genetic Disorders — p.22–23
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 1-a: using karyotypes to diagnose genetic disorders — p.22–23

title: KP1: Karyotype preparation
searchText: kp1: karyotype preparation a karyotype can be made by collecting cells, stopping cell division during mitosis, staining chromosomes, and photographing them. describe how chromosome images are prepared for analysis.

title: KP2: Chromosome pairing and ordering
searchText: kp2: chromosome pairing and ordering chromosomes in a karyotype are paired, ordered from largest to smallest, and separated into autosomes and sex chromosomes. use chromosome size, shape, and sex chromosomes to organize a karyotype.

title: KP3: Detecting chromosome abnormalities
searchText: kp3: detecting chromosome abnormalities karyotypes can reveal abnormal chromosome number or structure, but not all single-gene mutations. use karyotype evidence to identify disorders such as trisomy 13, trisomy 18, or trisomy 21.

title: Chapter 1.2: How is hereditary information passed from one generation to the next?
searchText: chapter 1.2: how is hereditary information passed from one generation to the next?

title: 1.2 Concept 1: Genes pass on inherited traits from parent to offspring — p.26–30
searchText: 1.2 concept 1: genes pass on inherited traits from parent to offspring — p.26–30

title: KP1: Genetics and heredity
searchText: kp1: genetics and heredity genetics is the study of heredity, or the passing of traits from parents to offspring. use genetics vocabulary to explain how inherited characteristics move across generations.

title: KP2: Traits
searchText: kp2: traits a trait is an inherited characteristic, such as eye colour, hair colour, or flower colour. identify traits as observable results connected to inherited genetic information.

title: KP3: Mendel’s controlled crosses
searchText: kp3: mendel’s controlled crosses mendel used controlled breeding of pea plants to study how traits are inherited. explain why controlled crosses and careful record keeping made mendel’s experiments useful.

title: KP4: True-breeding plants and generations
searchText: kp4: true-breeding plants and generations true-breeding plants consistently produce offspring with one form of a trait; \(f 1\) and \(f 2\) refer to first and second generations. track how a trait may disappear in \(f 1\) and reappear in \(f 2\).

title: KP5: Law of segregation
searchText: kp5: law of segregation alleles for each inherited trait separate during gamete formation; each gamete carries one allele. connect meiosis, gametes, and fertilization to inheritance patterns.

title: KP6: Dominant and recessive alleles
searchText: kp6: dominant and recessive alleles a dominant allele is expressed when present; a recessive allele is expressed only when two recessive alleles are present. use uppercase and lowercase allele symbols to represent inheritance.

title: KP7: Genotype, phenotype, homozygous, and heterozygous
searchText: kp7: genotype, phenotype, homozygous, and heterozygous genotype is the allele combination; phenotype is the expressed trait; homozygous means two same alleles; heterozygous means two different alleles. distinguish what an organism looks like from the alleles it carries.

title: 1.2 Concept 2: Punnett squares show the probability of offspring inheriting specific traits — p.31–32
searchText: 1.2 concept 2: punnett squares show the probability of offspring inheriting specific traits — p.31–32

title: KP1: Genetic cross
searchText: kp1: genetic cross a genetic cross is deliberate breeding between a genetic male and a genetic female to produce offspring with genetic material from both parents. describe the parent genotypes involved in an inheritance problem.

title: KP2: Monohybrid cross
searchText: kp2: monohybrid cross a monohybrid cross studies inheritance of one trait. identify when a punnett square should focus on one gene or trait.

title: KP3: Punnett square structure
searchText: kp3: punnett square structure a punnett square is a grid that shows possible offspring genotypes based on parent gametes. place possible alleles from each parent along the top and side of the grid.

title: KP4: Genotypic ratios
searchText: kp4: genotypic ratios a genotypic ratio compares the predicted allele combinations among offspring. express possible offspring genotypes as ratios or fractions.

title: KP5: Phenotypic ratios and probability
searchText: kp5: phenotypic ratios and probability a phenotypic ratio compares predicted physical traits; probability describes the chance of each outcome. interpret punnett square results as probabilities, not guarantees.

title: 1.2 Concept 3: Both alleles are expressed in codominance — p.33–34
searchText: 1.2 concept 3: both alleles are expressed in codominance — p.33–34

title: KP1: Codominance
searchText: kp1: codominance in codominance, both alleles for a trait are fully expressed in a heterozygote. recognize phenotypes where two traits appear together instead of one hiding the other.

title: KP2: Codominant allele notation
searchText: kp2: codominant allele notation codominant alleles can be written with one capital gene letter and different superscript letters. read and write genotypes such as \(h r h w\) for codominant traits.

title: KP3: Roan animals
searchText: kp3: roan animals a roan animal shows both base colour and white hair because both alleles are expressed. use roan coat colour as a visible model of codominance.

title: KP4: Sickle cell anemia and hemoglobin
searchText: kp4: sickle cell anemia and hemoglobin sickle cell anemia is linked to an allele that affects hemoglobin, a protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen. connect allele differences to protein structure, cell shape, and body function.

title: KP5: Sickle cell trait
searchText: kp5: sickle cell trait individuals with genotype \(hb a hb s\) have both normal and sickled red blood cells. explain how heterozygotes can show both allele effects.

title: KP6: Heterozygote advantage
searchText: kp6: heterozygote advantage in some environments, the sickle cell trait can provide resistance to malaria. connect inheritance patterns to survival advantage in specific environments.

title: 1.2 Concept 4: In incomplete dominance, alleles are neither dominant nor recessive — p.35
searchText: 1.2 concept 4: in incomplete dominance, alleles are neither dominant nor recessive — p.35

title: KP1: Incomplete dominance
searchText: kp1: incomplete dominance in incomplete dominance, neither allele completely hides the other. identify inheritance patterns where the heterozygote has an intermediate phenotype.

title: KP2: Intermediate phenotype
searchText: kp2: intermediate phenotype a heterozygote in incomplete dominance shows a phenotype between the two homozygous phenotypes. recognize blended-looking traits as possible incomplete dominance.

title: KP3: Incomplete dominance ratios
searchText: kp3: incomplete dominance ratios a heterozygote × heterozygote cross can produce a \(1:2:1\) phenotypic ratio. use punnett squares to predict all three phenotypes.

title: KP4: Superscript notation
searchText: kp4: superscript notation incomplete dominance can use superscripts, such as \(c r\) and \(c w\), to represent allele forms. avoid using lowercase symbols unless an allele is truly recessive.

title: KP5: Incomplete dominance versus codominance
searchText: kp5: incomplete dominance versus codominance incomplete dominance gives an intermediate phenotype; codominance shows both alleles fully expressed. classify inheritance patterns by how the heterozygote appears.

title: 1.2 Concept 5: Some inherited traits are due to alleles on the sex chromosomes — p.36
searchText: 1.2 concept 5: some inherited traits are due to alleles on the sex chromosomes — p.36

title: KP1: Sex-linked traits
searchText: kp1: sex-linked traits sex-linked traits are controlled by genes located on sex chromosomes. identify when inheritance depends on \(x\) or \(y\) chromosomes.

title: KP2: X-linked traits
searchText: kp2: x-linked traits traits controlled by genes on the \(x\) chromosome are called x-linked traits. track x-linked alleles differently in genetic males and genetic females.

title: KP3: Recessive X-linked inheritance
searchText: kp3: recessive x-linked inheritance genetic males are affected by recessive x-linked traits more often because they have only one \(x\) chromosome. explain why some recessive traits appear more often in males.

title: KP4: Carriers
searchText: kp4: carriers a genetic female can carry a recessive x-linked allele on one \(x\) chromosome without expressing the trait. use carrier genotypes to explain how recessive x-linked traits pass through families.

title: KP5: Red-green colour vision deficiency
searchText: kp5: red-green colour vision deficiency red-green colour vision deficiency is a recessive x-linked trait. use \(x b\), \(x b\), and \(y\) notation to model sex-linked inheritance.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Hemophilia — p.37
searchText: optional extension — at issue: hemophilia — p.37

title: KP1: Pedigrees
searchText: kp1: pedigrees a pedigree is a flowchart that uses symbols to show relationships and inherited traits across generations. use pedigrees to trace inheritance patterns in a family.

title: KP2: Hemophilia as an X-linked disorder
searchText: kp2: hemophilia as an x-linked disorder hemophilia is a recessive x-linked disorder in which blood does not clot properly. connect carrier females, affected males, and x-linked inheritance.

title: KP3: Pedigree reasoning
searchText: kp3: pedigree reasoning pedigrees and punnett squares can be combined to infer likely genotypes. analyze whether a person is affected, a carrier, or unaffected.

title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Be Inspired — Dr. Nadine Caron — p.38
searchText: optional extension — make a difference: be inspired — dr. nadine caron — p.38

title: KP1: Genomics and healthcare
searchText: kp1: genomics and healthcare genomics can compare dna sequences from a patient with genetic disease to a background population. explain how dna sequence comparison can support medical understanding.

title: KP2: Indigenous representation in genomics
searchText: kp2: indigenous representation in genomics the silent genomes project addresses the relative absence of indigenous canadians in genomics. connect genetics research to equity, consent, and community participation.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 1-B: Understanding Probability — p.40
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 1-b: understanding probability — p.40

title: KP1: Probability and inheritance
searchText: kp1: probability and inheritance probability is the chance that a given event will occur. use probability to predict possible inheritance outcomes.

title: KP2: Coin-flip model
searchText: kp2: coin-flip model a coin flip can model chance events, but every model has limits. compare random coin outcomes with allele separation during meiosis.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 1-C: Corn Genetics — p.41
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 1-c: corn genetics — p.41

title: KP1: Kernels as offspring
searchText: kp1: kernels as offspring each corn kernel is a separate offspring formed from an egg and a pollen grain. use corn kernels to observe inheritance patterns in many offspring.

title: KP2: F₂ phenotypic ratio
searchText: kp2: f₂ phenotypic ratio counting visible traits in the \(f 2\) generation can test a predicted phenotypic ratio. compare observed trait counts with theoretical inheritance predictions.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 1-D: Performing a Monohybrid Cross — p.42–43
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 1-d: performing a monohybrid cross — p.42–43

title: KP1: Modelling alleles
searchText: kp1: modelling alleles physical objects can represent alleles in a model of a monohybrid cross. use models to simulate how alleles combine during fertilization.

title: KP2: F₁ and F₂ crosses
searchText: kp2: f₁ and f₂ crosses a true-breeding cross can produce \(f 1\) offspring, and crossing \(f 1\) offspring can produce \(f 2\) patterns. compare model results with mendel-style inheritance patterns.

title: KP3: Sample size and reliability
searchText: kp3: sample size and reliability larger numbers of trials usually produce results closer to expected ratios. explain why class data or large datasets are more reliable than a small number of crosses.

title: Chapter 1.3: How can natural and artificial selection influence changes in populations?
searchText: chapter 1.3: how can natural and artificial selection influence changes in populations?

title: 1.3 Concept 1: DNA mutations produce genetic diversity within a population — p.46
searchText: 1.3 concept 1: dna mutations produce genetic diversity within a population — p.46

title: KP1: Mutation
searchText: kp1: mutation a mutation is a permanent change in the dna of an organism. explain how mutations can create new alleles and new genetic variation.

title: KP2: Genetic variation in populations
searchText: kp2: genetic variation in populations variation among individuals in a population is largely the result of differences in genes. connect inherited gene differences to variation within a species.

title: KP3: Sources of mutations
searchText: kp3: sources of mutations mutations can occur spontaneously during dna copying or result from environmental agents. identify how dna changes can arise before a cell divides or from outside factors.

title: KP4: Effects of mutations
searchText: kp4: effects of mutations mutations may be harmful, neutral, or beneficial. avoid assuming that every mutation is harmful.

title: KP5: Somatic cell mutations
searchText: kp5: somatic cell mutations a mutation in a body cell disappears from the population when the organism dies. distinguish non-inherited body cell mutations from inherited genetic changes.

title: KP6: Gamete mutations
searchText: kp6: gamete mutations a mutation in a gamete may be passed to future generations as a new allele. explain why inherited mutations are important for long-term population change.

title: 1.3 Concept 2: Natural selection favours traits that make an organism better suited to its environment — p.47–49
searchText: 1.3 concept 2: natural selection favours traits that make an organism better suited to its environment — p.47–49

title: KP1: Selective advantage
searchText: kp1: selective advantage a selective advantage is a genetic advantage that improves an organism’s chance of survival and reproduction. identify traits that help organisms survive under specific environmental conditions.

title: KP2: Natural selection
searchText: kp2: natural selection natural selection changes the characteristics of a population over many generations as organisms with heritable traits survive and reproduce. explain how inherited traits can become more common in a population.

title: KP3: Adaptation
searchText: kp3: adaptation an adaptation is a structural, behavioural, or physiological feature that helps an organism survive and reproduce. classify examples of adaptations by how they improve survival or reproduction.

title: KP4: Genetic diversity and selection
searchText: kp4: genetic diversity and selection natural selection requires variation within a species or population. explain why populations with genetic diversity have more possible responses to environmental change.

title: KP5: Natural selection acts on populations
searchText: kp5: natural selection acts on populations individuals do not change during their lifetime; populations change over generations. avoid saying that an individual organism evolves during its life.

title: KP6: Selective pressure
searchText: kp6: selective pressure selective pressure is an environmental condition that favours some traits and selects against others. identify abiotic and biotic selective pressures such as antibiotics, light levels, predators, parasites, and competition.

title: KP7: Natural selection is situational
searchText: kp7: natural selection is situational natural selection has no purpose or direction; the usefulness of a trait depends on the environment. explain why a trait can be helpful in one situation but not in another.

title: 1.3 Concept 3: Natural selection can lead to the formation of new species — p.50–53
searchText: 1.3 concept 3: natural selection can lead to the formation of new species — p.50–53

title: KP1: Species
searchText: kp1: species a species is a population or group of populations whose members can interbreed and produce fertile offspring. use reproductive ability to distinguish whether groups belong to the same species.

title: KP2: Speciation
searchText: kp2: speciation speciation occurs when members of a population change enough that they can no longer produce fertile offspring with the original population. explain how one species can split into two or more species over time.

title: KP3: Geographic isolation
searchText: kp3: geographic isolation a population can be split by a barrier such as a glacier, lava flow, ocean-level change, or movement to a separate habitat. connect isolation to separate gene pools and different evolutionary paths.

title: KP4: Adaptive radiation
searchText: kp4: adaptive radiation adaptive radiation is the diversification of a common ancestral species into differently adapted species. use island species, such as galapagos finches, as examples of adaptive radiation.

title: KP5: Mutations and natural selection in speciation
searchText: kp5: mutations and natural selection in speciation mutations create variation, and natural selection can favour different traits in different environments. explain how isolated populations become increasingly different over many generations.

title: KP6: Extinction
searchText: kp6: extinction extinction occurs when a species completely disappears from earth. connect environmental change and selective pressure to the loss of species.

title: KP7: Mass extinction
searchText: kp7: mass extinction mass extinction events are sharp declines in biodiversity caused by large-scale environmental changes. relate extinction events to major changes in ecosystems and species diversity.

title: 1.3 Concept 4: Environmental factors can cause mutations — p.54
searchText: 1.3 concept 4: environmental factors can cause mutations — p.54

title: KP1: Mutagen
searchText: kp1: mutagen a mutagen is a substance or event that increases the rate of mutation. identify environmental factors that can damage or change dna.

title: KP2: Physical mutagens
searchText: kp2: physical mutagens physical mutagens cause physical changes to dna structure. recognize x-rays and ultraviolet radiation as examples of physical mutagens.

title: KP3: Chemical mutagens
searchText: kp3: chemical mutagens chemical mutagens are molecules that can enter a cell nucleus and react with dna. recognize nitrites and gasoline fumes as examples of chemical mutagens.

title: KP4: Carcinogens
searchText: kp4: carcinogens a carcinogen is a substance or agent that causes cancer. connect ultraviolet radiation and cigarette smoke compounds to increased cancer risk.

title: KP5: Mutations and proteins
searchText: kp5: mutations and proteins a mutation can change gene instructions and affect the production of a specific protein. explain how dna changes may affect cell function and organism health.

title: 1.3 Concept 5: Humans select desired characteristics in organisms to be passed on to the next generation — p.55–57
searchText: 1.3 concept 5: humans select desired characteristics in organisms to be passed on to the next generation — p.55–57

title: KP1: Artificial selection
searchText: kp1: artificial selection artificial selection is selective pressure exerted by humans to improve or modify desirable traits. identify selective breeding in crops, livestock, companion animals, and domestic species.

title: KP2: Natural selection versus artificial selection
searchText: kp2: natural selection versus artificial selection in natural selection, environmental conditions select traits; in artificial selection, humans choose traits. compare how traits become more common in natural and human-directed contexts.

title: KP3: Artificial selection in animals
searchText: kp3: artificial selection in animals humans breed animals for traits such as milk production, growth rate, egg production, or appearance. explain how breeders change populations over generations by choosing parents.

title: KP4: Artificial selection in food crops
searchText: kp4: artificial selection in food crops many food crops are the result of long-term selective breeding for useful plant parts or traits. use wild mustard and its crop varieties as examples of selected traits.

title: KP5: Limits of selective breeding
searchText: kp5: limits of selective breeding breeding for one desired trait can reduce tolerance for other environmental conditions. explain why crop breeding must balance productivity with genetic variation.

title: KP6: Consequences of artificial selection
searchText: kp6: consequences of artificial selection artificial selection can have positive and negative effects, including health problems or reduced genetic diversity. evaluate benefits and risks of breeding organisms for specific traits.

title: KP7: Monoculture
searchText: kp7: monoculture monoculture is the repeated planting of the same varieties of a species over large areas. explain why low genetic diversity can make crops vulnerable to disease or environmental change.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: How can selection pressure lead to reverse speciation? — p.58–59
searchText: optional extension — at issue: how can selection pressure lead to reverse speciation? — p.58–59

title: KP1: Reverse speciation
searchText: kp1: reverse speciation reverse speciation occurs when two species that diverged from a common ancestor become replaced by one hybrid species. use enos lake sticklebacks as an example of species merging through interbreeding.

title: KP2: Human-caused selection pressure
searchText: kp2: human-caused selection pressure introduced species can change how existing species interact, breed, and survive. explain how an invasive crayfish may have changed stickleback nesting, feeding, or breeding patterns.

title: KP3: Biodiversity impact
searchText: kp3: biodiversity impact reverse speciation can create a new hybrid population while reducing the number of distinct species. evaluate why “new species formation” does not always mean biodiversity increases.

title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take a Stand — Investigate Polycultures and other Sustainable Food Systems — p.60–61
searchText: optional extension — make a difference: take a stand — investigate polycultures and other sustainable food systems — p.60–61

title: KP1: Monoculture consequences
searchText: kp1: monoculture consequences large-scale monoculture can increase food production but may cause environmental, economic, and social problems. identify consequences such as topsoil loss, pollution, higher chemical use, and working-condition concerns.

title: KP2: Sustainable agriculture
searchText: kp2: sustainable agriculture sustainable agriculture meets present needs while supporting the health of land, human ecosystems, and natural ecosystems for the future. evaluate food systems using environmental, economic, and social criteria.

title: KP3: Polyculture
searchText: kp3: polyculture polyculture grows a diversity of crops on the same plot of land. compare polyculture with monoculture in terms of biodiversity and resilience.

title: KP4: Companion planting
searchText: kp4: companion planting companion planting places plant species near each other to gain benefits such as pest control, nutrient support, higher yield, or soil moisture retention. use the “three sisters” planting system as an example of plant relationships that support sustainability.

title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take Action — The Sixth Extinction — p.62
searchText: optional extension — make a difference: take action — the sixth extinction — p.62

title: KP1: Sixth mass extinction
searchText: kp1: sixth mass extinction scientists warn that earth may be approaching a sixth mass extinction. connect current biodiversity loss to human activities rather than only natural events.

title: KP2: Human impacts on extinction risk
searchText: kp2: human impacts on extinction risk habitat destruction, land degradation, and over-exploitation put many species at risk. identify human behaviours that increase extinction pressure.

title: KP3: Conservation action
searchText: kp3: conservation action conservation efforts can include evaluating species status, protecting habitat, and restoring populations. design or assess a campaign to protect a species at risk.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 1-E: Variations Great and Small — p.64–65
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 1-e: variations great and small — p.64–65

title: KP1: Measuring variation
searchText: kp1: measuring variation variation within a species can be studied by measuring traits in individuals. use measurements such as hand size or seed mass to compare variation in populations.

title: KP2: Frequency distributions
searchText: kp2: frequency distributions many traits vary continuously and can form a bell-shaped distribution. use histograms to analyze patterns in measured trait data.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 1-F: Modelling Adaptation — p.66
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 1-f: modelling adaptation — p.66

title: KP1: Modelling beak adaptation
searchText: kp1: modelling beak adaptation different tools can model different bird beak structures. compare how different structures perform with different food types.

title: KP2: Environmental change and food availability
searchText: kp2: environmental change and food availability changes such as drought or flood can alter which food types are available. predict how environmental change may favour some traits over others.

title: KP3: Model limitations
searchText: kp3: model limitations a model can show patterns of adaptation but cannot include every feature of a real ecosystem. evaluate what a model shows well and what it leaves out.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 1-G: Leopard Frogs—One Species or Eight? — p.67
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 1-g: leopard frogs—one species or eight? — p.67

title: KP1: Behavioural isolation
searchText: kp1: behavioural isolation differences in mating signals, such as calls or songs, can prevent interbreeding. explain how similar-looking organisms may still be separate species.

title: KP2: Conservation and species identification
searchText: kp2: conservation and species identification correctly identifying species can affect conservation and monitoring decisions. explain why recognizing multiple leopard frog species matters for protecting amphibian populations.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 1-H: Islands and Species — p.68–69
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 1-h: islands and species — p.68–69

title: KP1: Island adaptation
searchText: kp1: island adaptation isolated island environments can favour different adaptations based on food, predators, geography, and climate. design traits that would help a species survive in a specific island environment.

title: KP2: Adaptation and gene pools
searchText: kp2: adaptation and gene pools a new individual can affect a population only if it survives, reproduces, and adds alleles to the gene pool. explain how migration between islands could affect future population change.

title: Chapter 1.4: How and why are the genes of organisms manipulated?
searchText: chapter 1.4: how and why are the genes of organisms manipulated?

title: 1.4 Concept 1: DNA of a living cell can be copied, modified, and inserted into another organism — p.72–75
searchText: 1.4 concept 1: dna of a living cell can be copied, modified, and inserted into another organism — p.72–75

title: KP1: Biotechnology
searchText: kp1: biotechnology biotechnology is the use of technology and organisms to produce useful products. identify gene manipulation as a scientific and technological application of dna knowledge.

title: KP2: Cloning
searchText: kp2: cloning cloning is a process that produces identical copies of genes, cells, or organisms. distinguish gene cloning from cloning an entire organism.

title: KP3: Gene cloning
searchText: kp3: gene cloning gene cloning manipulates dna to produce many copies of a gene or dna segment in foreign cells. explain how cloned genes can be used for study or for producing proteins such as insulin.

title: KP4: Vectors and plasmids
searchText: kp4: vectors and plasmids a vector carries dna to be cloned into a foreign cell; a plasmid is a common bacterial vector. describe the role of a plasmid in moving a gene into a bacterial cell.

title: KP5: Recombinant DNA
searchText: kp5: recombinant dna recombinant dna is a dna molecule that includes genetic material from different sources. explain how enzymes can help cut and join dna pieces during gene cloning.

title: KP6: Transformation
searchText: kp6: transformation transformation occurs when a foreign cell takes in recombinant dna. connect transformation to the production of many copies of a cloned gene or dna fragment.

title: KP7: Transgenic organisms
searchText: kp7: transgenic organisms a transgenic organism contains foreign dna introduced into its genome through genetic engineering. recognize transgenic plants and animals as types of genetically modified organisms, or gmos.

title: 1.4 Concept 2: DNA technology has many uses — p.76–79
searchText: 1.4 concept 2: dna technology has many uses — p.76–79

title: KP1: DNA profiles
searchText: kp1: dna profiles a dna profile determines sequences of dna regions that are unique to an individual. explain how dna profiles can be used in law enforcement or family relationship testing.

title: KP2: Artificial insemination
searchText: kp2: artificial insemination artificial insemination collects and concentrates sperm before placing it in the female’s uterus. identify artificial insemination as a reproductive technology.

title: KP3: In vitro fertilization
searchText: kp3: in vitro fertilization in vitro fertilization, or ivf, fertilizes eggs with sperm outside the body before placing embryos in the uterus. compare ivf with artificial insemination.

title: KP4: Gene therapy
searchText: kp4: gene therapy gene therapy is an experimental treatment that inserts a healthy form of a gene into affected cells or tissues. explain how a healthy gene may replace or compensate for a mutated gene.

title: KP5: Genetic disease diagnosis
searchText: kp5: genetic disease diagnosis identifying mutated genes can help link genetic material to disease. connect dna technology to improved diagnosis and prediction of genetic disorders.

title: KP6: DNA technology in British Columbia
searchText: kp6: dna technology in british columbia dna-based technologies can test plants and biological samples for viruses, invasive species, or pathogens. explain how dna testing can support agriculture, trade, forests, and ecosystem protection.

title: 1.4 Concept 3: The use of biotechnology has some risks and raises some ethical issues — p.80–81
searchText: 1.4 concept 3: the use of biotechnology has some risks and raises some ethical issues — p.80–81

title: KP1: GMO controversy
searchText: kp1: gmo controversy gmos have important benefits but also raise environmental, health, social, and ethical concerns. evaluate biotechnology using both benefits and risks.

title: KP2: Environmental risks
searchText: kp2: environmental risks gmo-related risks may include stronger herbicide use, gene transfer to other species, and effects on wild populations. explain concerns about “superweeds,” “superbugs,” and ecological competition.

title: KP3: Health concerns
searchText: kp3: health concerns some people are concerned about long-term effects, allergic reactions, and safety of genetically modified food or medicine. connect gmo regulation and labelling to informed consumer decisions.

title: KP4: Social and economic issues
searchText: kp4: social and economic issues biotechnology may affect research funding, world hunger, private enterprise, and global food markets. analyze how biotechnology can influence society beyond the laboratory.

title: KP5: Ethics of using organisms
searchText: kp5: ethics of using organisms some biotechnology uses other species mainly for human benefit. evaluate ethical questions about transgenic animals, medical products, and organ-donor research.

title: KP6: Gene therapy concerns
searchText: kp6: gene therapy concerns gene therapy has promise but can produce negative results and raises questions about which conditions should be treated. discuss why safety, regulation, and definitions of “disorder” matter in gene therapy.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Stem Cells: Paralysis Cured — p.82–83
searchText: optional extension — at issue: stem cells: paralysis cured — p.82–83

title: KP1: Stem cells
searchText: kp1: stem cells a stem cell can differentiate into many different cell types. explain why stem cells may help repair damaged tissues.

title: KP2: Embryonic and adult stem cells
searchText: kp2: embryonic and adult stem cells embryonic stem cells can differentiate into any cell type; adult stem cells are limited to certain cell types. compare stem cell types by their developmental potential.

title: KP3: Adult stem cell treatments
searchText: kp3: adult stem cell treatments adult stem cells may be grown and manipulated to generate specific cell types. connect adult stem cell research to possible treatments for heart damage, vision loss, diabetes, or spinal injury.

title: KP4: Stem cells and paralysis
searchText: kp4: stem cells and paralysis some adult stem cell research investigates replacing damaged nerve cells after spinal cord injury. evaluate the promise and limits of stem cell-based medical treatments.

title: Optional Extension — Science at Work: Biology Connections — p.84
searchText: optional extension — science at work: biology connections — p.84

title: KP1: Biology-related careers
searchText: kp1: biology-related careers biology knowledge supports careers such as bioethicist, plant pathologist, bioinformatics data scientist, soil scientist, and medical illustrator. connect dna, genetics, and biotechnology concepts to possible science careers.

title: KP2: Career knowledge and skills
searchText: kp2: career knowledge and skills biology careers may require scientific knowledge, communication, ethical reasoning, data analysis, or fieldwork. identify skills connected to work in genetics, ecology, health, or biotechnology.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 1-I: Owning and Controlling Genetic Information — p.86
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 1-i: owning and controlling genetic information — p.86

title: KP1: Genetic information privacy
searchText: kp1: genetic information privacy genetic information raises questions about privacy, ownership, and control. analyze why personal genetic data may need regulation.

title: KP2: Genetic discrimination
searchText: kp2: genetic discrimination genetic profiles could affect employment, insurance, medical plans, criminal cases, paternity testing, biobanks, or research studies. identify situations where genetic information could be misused.

title: KP3: Regulation and policy
searchText: kp3: regulation and policy laws and policies may struggle to keep up with scientific advances in genetics. propose rules for responsible control and sharing of genetic information.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 1-J: The Effects of Biotechnology — p.87
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 1-j: the effects of biotechnology — p.87

title: KP1: GMO benefits and purposes
searchText: kp1: gmo benefits and purposes gmos may be developed to improve agriculture, medicine, food production, or environmental management. identify why a specific gmo was developed and what advantage it provides.

title: KP2: GMO environmental effects
searchText: kp2: gmo environmental effects gmo use can have positive and negative effects on biodiversity, non-target organisms, and ecosystem interactions. compare environmental benefits and risks of different gmo examples.

title: KP3: Evidence quality
searchText: kp3: evidence quality claims about gmos should be judged by source credibility, evidence quality, and whether more data are needed. distinguish evidence-based reports from misconceptions or unsupported opinions.

title: Unit 2: Chemical processes require energy change as atoms are rearranged
searchText: unit 2: chemical processes require energy change as atoms are rearranged

title: Chapter 2.1: How are chemical processes part of our lives?
searchText: chapter 2.1: how are chemical processes part of our lives?

title: 2.1 Concept 1: Applications of chemistry are everywhere in the world around you — p.104–105
searchText: 2.1 concept 1: applications of chemistry are everywhere in the world around you — p.104–105

title: KP1: Chemical reactions
searchText: kp1: chemical reactions a chemical reaction is a process in which atoms of one or more substances are rearranged to form different substances. identify chemical reactions in visible events such as fireworks and in less obvious everyday processes.

title: KP2: Chemistry in living systems
searchText: kp2: chemistry in living systems chemical reactions help maintain health and sustain life. connect chemistry to food digestion, nutrients, body cells, and living processes.

title: KP3: Chemistry in medicine
searchText: kp3: chemistry in medicine chemistry supports medical tools and technologies that help visualize or understand the body. explain how chemical knowledge can contribute to medical advances such as imaging technologies.

title: KP4: Chemistry in personal and consumer products
searchText: kp4: chemistry in personal and consumer products personal care products and many consumer materials are designed with specific chemical properties. analyze how chemistry affects the production, use, and safety of everyday products.

title: KP5: Chemistry in transportation and pollution
searchText: kp5: chemistry in transportation and pollution transportation technologies depend on chemistry but can also contribute to air, land, and water pollution. evaluate both positive and negative effects of chemistry in transportation.

title: KP6: Synthetic materials
searchText: kp6: synthetic materials synthetic materials such as plastics and synthetic fibres are human-made products with designed properties. compare the advantages of durability and usefulness with disadvantages such as persistence and pollution.

title: 2.1 Concept 2: Knowing how to handle chemicals helps keep us and our environment safe — p.108–110
searchText: 2.1 concept 2: knowing how to handle chemicals helps keep us and our environment safe — p.108–110

title: KP1: Chemical hazards
searchText: kp1: chemical hazards some chemical compounds have properties that make them potentially dangerous. recognize that safe handling depends on understanding the chemical’s risks.

title: KP2: WHMIS labels
searchText: kp2: whmis labels whmis labels provide information about chemical hazards in laboratories and workplaces. use whmis symbols to identify risks such as flammability, corrosion, toxicity, or pressure hazards.

title: KP3: Safety Data Sheets
searchText: kp3: safety data sheets safety data sheets, or sds, provide information about chemical properties, handling, storage, disposal, hazards, and first aid. consult sds information before planning or carrying out chemical work.

title: KP4: Protective equipment and behaviour
searchText: kp4: protective equipment and behaviour safe chemical work requires protective equipment and responsible behaviour. use safety goggles, lab coats or aprons, tied-back hair, and no eating or drinking during investigations.

title: KP5: Emergency response in the classroom
searchText: kp5: emergency response in the classroom chemical safety includes knowing emergency equipment, exits, eyewash stations, and spill-response procedures. respond appropriately to spills, eye exposure, skin contact, and other classroom hazards.

title: KP6: Heating, fumes, and handling containers
searchText: kp6: heating, fumes, and handling containers heating chemicals and handling fumes require extra caution. point heated containers away from people, never leave flames unattended, and waft fumes instead of smelling directly.

title: KP7: Minimizing personal and environmental risk
searchText: kp7: minimizing personal and environmental risk chemical safety includes reducing risks to people and reducing harm to the environment. choose handling, storage, and disposal methods that protect both personal safety and environmental quality.

title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take Action — Chemicals in Our Water — p.106–107
searchText: optional extension — make a difference: take action — chemicals in our water — p.106–107

title: KP1: Water as a solvent
searchText: kp1: water as a solvent water is called the universal solvent because many substances can dissolve in it. explain why water can contain both naturally occurring substances and pollutants.

title: KP2: Water pollution sources
searchText: kp2: water pollution sources water pollution can come from household, mining, industrial, pharmaceutical, and plastic waste. identify human activities that introduce harmful substances into water systems.

title: KP3: Wastewater and stormwater
searchText: kp3: wastewater and stormwater wastewater treatment can reduce but not remove all pollutants; stormwater can flow directly into local waterways. compare treated wastewater with untreated storm drain runoff.

title: KP4: Safe drinking water and community responsibility
searchText: kp4: safe drinking water and community responsibility water quality issues affect communities differently, including some first nations communities with unsafe drinking water. develop actions that improve awareness, reduce pollution, or support cleaner water systems.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Fighting Fires with Chemistry — p.112–113
searchText: optional extension — at issue: fighting fires with chemistry — p.112–113

title: KP1: Fire extinguisher types
searchText: kp1: fire extinguisher types different classes of fires require different types of fire extinguishers. match extinguisher type to fuel source, such as ordinary combustibles, flammable liquids, electrical equipment, metals, or cooking oils.

title: KP2: Correct extinguisher choice
searchText: kp2: correct extinguisher choice the wrong extinguisher can spread a fire or create new hazards. explain why water should not be used on electrical or oil-based fires.

title: KP3: Fire extinguisher labels
searchText: kp3: fire extinguisher labels fire extinguishers use letters and symbols to show which fire classes they can safely address. read extinguisher labels before deciding how to respond to a fire.

title: KP4: Chemical trade-offs in fire safety
searchText: kp4: chemical trade-offs in fire safety fire-suppression chemicals can have benefits and environmental or health risks. evaluate why some chemicals, such as halons, may be phased out or require careful disposal.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-A: Is It Worth It? — p.114–115
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 2-a: is it worth it? — p.114–115

title: KP1: Product life cycle
searchText: kp1: product life cycle a product’s life cycle includes raw material extraction, processing, manufacture, distribution, use, recycling, and disposal. analyze the environmental and social impact of a synthetic product across its life cycle.

title: KP2: Positive and negative product impacts
searchText: kp2: positive and negative product impacts synthetic products can benefit society while also producing unintended environmental consequences. compare usefulness, durability, cost, waste, toxicity, and pollution for one product.

title: KP3: Green alternatives and recycling
searchText: kp3: green alternatives and recycling negative impacts can sometimes be reduced through recycling programs, reuse, or greener alternatives. evaluate whether a synthetic product is “worth it” based on evidence.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-B: Prepare an Emergency Response Plan — p.116–117
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 2-b: prepare an emergency response plan — p.116–117

title: KP1: Emergency preparedness
searchText: kp1: emergency preparedness chemical accidents can threaten people and the environment, so planning reduces harm. identify possible emergency scenarios involving gas leaks, fires, spills, or evacuation needs.

title: KP2: Response plan components
searchText: kp2: response plan components an emergency response plan should include preparation, evacuation procedures, communication, cleanup, and disposal. build a plan that explains who acts, where people go, and how hazards are managed.

title: KP3: Communication and audience
searchText: kp3: communication and audience emergency plans must be communicated clearly to the people who need to follow them. present emergency information in a format appropriate for a school, workplace, or community.

title: Chapter 2.2: What happens to atoms in a chemical reaction?
searchText: chapter 2.2: what happens to atoms in a chemical reaction?

title: 2.2 Concept 1: Atoms bond together to form ionic and covalent compounds — p.120–122
searchText: 2.2 concept 1: atoms bond together to form ionic and covalent compounds — p.120–122

title: KP1: Compounds
searchText: kp1: compounds compounds are made of atoms of different elements chemically combined in specific proportions. classify substances as elements or compounds before analyzing a chemical reaction.

title: KP2: Ionic compounds
searchText: kp2: ionic compounds ionic compounds are made of positively charged cations and negatively charged anions. identify ionic compounds as substances formed from oppositely charged ions.

title: KP3: Ionic bonds
searchText: kp3: ionic bonds an ionic bond is a strong attraction between oppositely charged ions. explain how electron transfer between metal and non-metal atoms forms stable ions.

title: KP4: Covalent compounds
searchText: kp4: covalent compounds covalent compounds form when atoms of non-metal elements share electrons. distinguish covalent bonding from ionic bonding.

title: KP5: Covalent bonds
searchText: kp5: covalent bonds a covalent bond is a strong attraction formed when atoms share valence electrons. identify single, double, and triple covalent bonds in simple molecules.

title: KP6: Molecules
searchText: kp6: molecules a molecule is the smallest independent unit of a covalent compound or a covalently bonded element. distinguish molecular substances from ionic crystal lattices.

title: KP7: Formula units versus molecules
searchText: kp7: formula units versus molecules ionic compounds are represented by formula units, while most covalent compounds exist as molecules. avoid describing ionic compounds such as sodium chloride as separate molecules.

title: 2.2 Concept 2: Bonds are broken, atoms are rearranged, and new bonds are formed — p.123–124
searchText: 2.2 concept 2: bonds are broken, atoms are rearranged, and new bonds are formed — p.123–124

title: KP1: Atom rearrangement
searchText: kp1: atom rearrangement in a chemical reaction, atoms are rearranged to form new substances. track atoms from reactants to products.

title: KP2: Breaking chemical bonds
searchText: kp2: breaking chemical bonds existing chemical bonds must be broken before atoms can rearrange. explain why energy is needed to separate bonded atoms or ions.

title: KP3: Forming new chemical bonds
searchText: kp3: forming new chemical bonds new bonds form between rearranged atoms to produce new substances. identify product formation as evidence that new bonds have formed.

title: KP4: Bond energy changes
searchText: kp4: bond energy changes breaking bonds requires energy; forming bonds releases energy. connect chemical reactions to energy changes.

title: KP5: Stability and valence shells
searchText: kp5: stability and valence shells bond formation can make atoms more stable by filling valence shells. explain why atoms form ionic or covalent bonds.

title: 2.2 Concept 3: Mass cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction — p.125–126
searchText: 2.2 concept 3: mass cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction — p.125–126

title: KP1: Law of conservation of mass
searchText: kp1: law of conservation of mass in a chemical reaction, the total mass of substances used equals the total mass of substances produced. explain why atoms are conserved during a chemical reaction.

title: KP2: Closed systems
searchText: kp2: closed systems in a closed system, energy can enter or leave, but matter cannot. use closed systems to test whether mass is conserved.

title: KP3: Open systems
searchText: kp3: open systems in an open system, both matter and energy can enter or leave. explain why gases escaping from a reaction can make mass measurements appear to change.

title: KP4: Lavoisier’s contribution
searchText: kp4: lavoisier’s contribution careful mass measurements before and after reactions helped establish the law of conservation of mass. connect experimental evidence to scientific laws.

title: KP5: Atom conservation
searchText: kp5: atom conservation atoms are not created or destroyed in chemical reactions; they are rearranged. use atom counting to check whether a chemical equation obeys conservation of mass.

title: 2.2 Concept 4: A chemical equation represents what happens to the atoms in a reaction — p.128–133
searchText: 2.2 concept 4: a chemical equation represents what happens to the atoms in a reaction — p.128–133

title: KP1: Chemical equations
searchText: kp1: chemical equations a chemical equation uses words or chemical formulas to represent a chemical reaction. interpret the arrow as “produces” or “yields” and plus signs as “reacts with” or “and.”

title: KP2: Reactants and products
searchText: kp2: reactants and products reactants are substances that undergo chemical change; products are substances formed in the reaction. identify reactants on the left side and products on the right side of a chemical equation.

title: KP3: Word equations
searchText: kp3: word equations a word equation describes reactants and products using names. translate a reaction description into a word equation before using formulas.

title: KP4: Skeleton equations
searchText: kp4: skeleton equations a skeleton equation uses chemical formulas but may not show correct reactant-product proportions. recognize that a skeleton equation may not obey conservation of mass.

title: KP5: Balanced chemical equations
searchText: kp5: balanced chemical equations a balanced chemical equation has the same number of atoms of each element on both sides. use conservation of mass to check whether an equation is balanced.

title: KP6: Coefficients
searchText: kp6: coefficients a coefficient is placed in front of a chemical formula to show the ratio of substances in a balanced equation. balance equations by changing coefficients, not subscripts.

title: KP7: Subscripts
searchText: kp7: subscripts a subscript in a chemical formula shows how many atoms of an element are present in one molecule or formula unit. avoid changing subscripts because this changes the identity of the substance.

title: KP8: States of matter
searchText: kp8: states of matter chemical equations may include state symbols such as \((s)\), \((l)\), \((g)\), and \((aq)\). include physical states when describing reactants and products.

title: KP9: Diatomic and polyatomic elements
searchText: kp9: diatomic and polyatomic elements some elements naturally exist as molecules such as \(h 2\), \(n 2\), \(o 2\), \(f 2\), \(cl 2\), \(br 2\), \(i 2\), \(s 8\), and \(p 4\). write correct formulas for elemental reactants or products.

title: KP10: Balancing strategy
searchText: kp10: balancing strategy balance compounds first, elements last, and treat unchanged polyatomic ions as single units when possible. check final atom counts on both sides of the equation.

title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take a Stand — What does the law of conservation of mass tell us about waste? — p.127
searchText: optional extension — make a difference: take a stand — what does the law of conservation of mass tell us about waste? — p.127

title: KP1: Matter and waste
searchText: kp1: matter and waste the law of conservation of mass means waste matter does not disappear when it is buried, dumped, burned, or released. connect chemical waste to long-term environmental responsibility.

title: KP2: Reducing versus recycling
searchText: kp2: reducing versus recycling reducing waste prevents material use, while recycling changes or reuses material already produced. explain why reducing waste is different from managing waste after it exists.

title: KP3: Waste classification
searchText: kp3: waste classification waste can be classified by danger level or source, such as hazardous, residential, municipal, agricultural, or industrial. evaluate how classification helps or limits environmental decision-making.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Can traditional methods be used to tan leather sustainably? — p.134
searchText: optional extension — at issue: can traditional methods be used to tan leather sustainably? — p.134

title: KP1: Tanning as chemical processing
searchText: kp1: tanning as chemical processing treating raw hide involves chemical processes that prevent decay and keep leather flexible and waterproof. connect traditional tanning knowledge to chemical change.

title: KP2: Brain tanning
searchText: kp2: brain tanning brain tanning uses fats and oils from brain tissue to change the chemical properties of hide. compare natural tanning processes with industrial chemical methods.

title: KP3: Sustainability trade-offs
searchText: kp3: sustainability trade-offs traditional tanning can use natural materials and more of the whole animal, while industrial tanning may use toxic chemicals such as chromium salts. evaluate tanning methods by sustainability, safety, scale, and environmental impact.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-C: The Law of Conservation of Mass — p.136–137
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 2-c: the law of conservation of mass — p.136–137

title: KP1: Testing conservation of mass
searchText: kp1: testing conservation of mass conservation of mass can be investigated by measuring reactant and product systems before and after reaction. compare mass changes in closed and open systems.

title: KP2: Closed-system reaction
searchText: kp2: closed-system reaction a sealed container prevents matter from escaping during a reaction. use a closed system to show that total mass remains constant.

title: KP3: Open-system reaction
searchText: kp3: open-system reaction an open container allows matter such as gas to leave the system. explain why measured mass can decrease when gas escapes.

title: KP4: Evidence of chemical reaction
searchText: kp4: evidence of chemical reaction observations such as colour change, precipitate formation, gas production, or temperature change can indicate a chemical reaction. record observations and connect them to reaction evidence.

title: Chapter 2.3: How is energy involved in chemical processes?
searchText: chapter 2.3: how is energy involved in chemical processes?

title: 2.3 Concept 1: Matter and energy interact in physical and chemical changes — p.140
searchText: 2.3 concept 1: matter and energy interact in physical and chemical changes — p.140

title: KP1: Matter and energy interaction
searchText: kp1: matter and energy interaction matter and energy continually interact in physical and chemical changes. identify energy transfers in everyday processes such as heating water, cooking food, or melting ice.

title: KP2: Energy input for change
searchText: kp2: energy input for change many changes require an input of energy to begin or continue. explain why boiling water, toasting bread, or igniting propane requires added energy.

title: KP3: Activation energy
searchText: kp3: activation energy activation energy is the minimum energy needed for a reaction to occur. describe activation energy as a barrier or “hill” that reactants must overcome.

title: KP4: Reactant collisions
searchText: kp4: reactant collisions reactants must collide with enough energy to begin breaking bonds. connect successful collisions to the start of a chemical reaction.

title: KP5: Initial energy and self-sustaining reactions
searchText: kp5: initial energy and self-sustaining reactions some reactions need an initial energy input, then continue because the reaction releases enough energy to keep going. explain why a propane torch needs a spark to start but can keep burning after ignition.

title: KP6: Energy in physical changes
searchText: kp6: energy in physical changes physical changes can involve energy transfer even when no new substance forms. explain melting ice as a process involving energy moving from warmer surroundings to the ice.

title: 2.3 Concept 2: Energy is transferred between chemical reactions and the surroundings — p.142–145
searchText: 2.3 concept 2: energy is transferred between chemical reactions and the surroundings — p.142–145

title: KP1: Energy changes in chemical reactions
searchText: kp1: energy changes in chemical reactions all chemical reactions are accompanied by changes in energy. connect photosynthesis and cellular respiration to energy stored or released through chemical reactions.

title: KP2: System and surroundings
searchText: kp2: system and surroundings the system is the reacting materials; the surroundings include everything outside the system. identify the reaction mixture as the system and the container, air, classroom, or observer as surroundings.

title: KP3: Conservation of energy
searchText: kp3: conservation of energy energy cannot be created or destroyed; energy leaving the system enters the surroundings, and energy entering the system comes from the surroundings. trace energy transfer during exothermic and endothermic reactions.

title: KP4: Exothermic reactions
searchText: kp4: exothermic reactions an exothermic reaction has a net release of energy to the surroundings. identify reactions where the surroundings warm up or light/heat is released.

title: KP5: Endothermic reactions
searchText: kp5: endothermic reactions an endothermic reaction has a net absorption of energy from the surroundings. identify reactions where the surroundings cool down or energy is absorbed.

title: KP6: Bond-breaking and bond-forming energy
searchText: kp6: bond-breaking and bond-forming energy breaking bonds absorbs energy; forming bonds releases energy. compare total energy absorbed in bond breaking with total energy released in bond forming.

title: KP7: Temperature as evidence
searchText: kp7: temperature as evidence temperature change can provide evidence for energy transfer in a reaction. use increasing temperature as evidence for exothermic change and decreasing temperature as evidence for endothermic change.

title: KP8: Energy-level diagrams
searchText: kp8: energy-level diagrams energy-level diagrams show the relative energy of reactants and products and the overall energy change. interpret higher-energy products as endothermic and lower-energy products as exothermic.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: How does energy change help to solve crimes? — p.141
searchText: optional extension — at issue: how does energy change help to solve crimes? — p.141

title: KP1: Luminol reaction
searchText: kp1: luminol reaction luminol can react with iron from hemoglobin to produce a glowing blue-green light. explain how chemical energy changes can help detect possible blood evidence.

title: KP2: Excited electrons and light
searchText: kp2: excited electrons and light energy absorbed by electrons can raise them to a higher energy level; light is released when they return to a lower energy level. connect electron energy changes to visible light production.

title: KP3: Limits of chemical evidence
searchText: kp3: limits of chemical evidence a positive luminol test is not conclusive because other substances can also make luminol emit light. explain why further testing, such as dna testing, may be needed after a luminol result.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Why does it matter how quickly a reaction occurs? — p.146
searchText: optional extension — at issue: why does it matter how quickly a reaction occurs? — p.146

title: KP1: Reaction rate
searchText: kp1: reaction rate every chemical reaction occurs at a certain rate under specific conditions. explain why some reactions, such as rusting, are useful when slow, while others must happen quickly.

title: KP2: Factors affecting reaction rate
searchText: kp2: factors affecting reaction rate temperature, surface area, concentration or amount of reactant, and catalysts can affect reaction rate. predict how changing conditions can speed up or slow down a reaction.

title: KP3: Catalysts
searchText: kp3: catalysts a catalyst speeds up a reaction without being used up. connect catalytic converters and enzymes to faster chemical reactions.

title: KP4: Runaway reactions
searchText: kp4: runaway reactions heat released by an exothermic reaction can increase reaction rate and cause an uncontrolled reaction. explain why industrial reactions need careful heat removal and safety controls.

title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Be Inspired — Ata Sina and the Shape-Shifting Paper — p.148
searchText: optional extension — make a difference: be inspired — ata sina and the shape-shifting paper — p.148

title: KP1: Thermoplastic polymers and energy
searchText: kp1: thermoplastic polymers and energy thermoplastic polymers can absorb energy, increase molecular motion, and change shape. explain how energy input can cause shape-shifting paper to fold into three-dimensional structures.

title: KP2: Chemistry and material design
searchText: kp2: chemistry and material design chemical properties of materials can be used to design lightweight, strong, and affordable structures. connect polymer chemistry to applications such as packaging, art materials, or toys.

title: KP3: Science and creative innovation
searchText: kp3: science and creative innovation chemistry can combine with art, design, and engineering to create new technologies. identify how personal interests can connect with scientific innovation.

title: Optional Extension — Science at Work: Chemistry Connections — p.149
searchText: optional extension — science at work: chemistry connections — p.149

title: KP1: Chemistry-related careers
searchText: kp1: chemistry-related careers chemistry knowledge supports careers such as mudlogger, forensic scientist, food scientist, materials technician, laboratory analyst, and fuel researcher. connect chemical reactions and material properties to real workplace tasks.

title: KP2: Chemistry and public safety
searchText: kp2: chemistry and public safety some chemistry careers involve protecting people, property, and the environment. explain how fire protection engineers or environmental assessors use chemistry knowledge.

title: KP3: Chemistry and sustainability
searchText: kp3: chemistry and sustainability fuel researchers study fuel performance, emissions, renewables, and alternatives. connect chemical processes to climate and energy decisions.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-D: Exothermic and Endothermic Reactions — p.150–151
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 2-d: exothermic and endothermic reactions — p.150–151

title: KP1: Temperature data and reaction type
searchText: kp1: temperature data and reaction type temperature measurements can show whether a reaction releases or absorbs energy overall. classify a reaction as exothermic or endothermic from temperature-change evidence.

title: KP2: Overall energy change
searchText: kp2: overall energy change overall energy change is the net result of energy absorbed to break bonds and energy released when new bonds form. use data and observations to support claims about energy transfer.

title: KP3: Reaction evidence
searchText: kp3: reaction evidence chemical reactions may show evidence such as temperature change, colour change, or formation of new substances. record observations and connect them to particle-level changes.

title: KP4: Particle-level explanation
searchText: kp4: particle-level explanation exothermic and endothermic classifications can be linked to atoms, ions, bonds broken, and bonds formed. explain what happens to atoms and ions during a reaction without relying only on visible changes.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-E: What Makes the Best Hand Warmer? — p.152
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 2-e: what makes the best hand warmer? — p.152

title: KP1: Dissolving and energy change
searchText: kp1: dissolving and energy change dissolving ionic compounds in water can be exothermic or endothermic. test temperature change to decide whether a substance could work as a hand warmer.

title: KP2: Design criteria
searchText: kp2: design criteria a useful hand warmer must balance temperature change, speed, safety, cost, volume, and environmental impact. use criteria to compare chemicals instead of choosing only the hottest reaction.

title: KP3: Safe chemical design
searchText: kp3: safe chemical design chemical products should be evaluated for both effectiveness and safety. include skin safety, handling safety, cleanup, and disposal in product design.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-F: Chemical Reactions as Energy Sources — p.153
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 2-f: chemical reactions as energy sources — p.153

title: KP1: Chemical reactions as energy sources
searchText: kp1: chemical reactions as energy sources society uses chemical reactions to produce useful energy for heating, transportation, cooking, and technology. compare fossil fuels and alternative energy sources such as hydrogen fuel cells.

title: KP2: Balanced equations and energy use
searchText: kp2: balanced equations and energy use energy-source analysis should include the chemical reaction and balanced chemical equation. connect atoms, bonds broken, bonds formed, and energy output in an energy-producing reaction.

title: KP3: Advantages and disadvantages
searchText: kp3: advantages and disadvantages energy sources have environmental, social, and economic benefits and drawbacks. evaluate an energy source by considering both energy usefulness and broader impacts.

title: Chapter 2.4: How do atoms rearrange in different types of chemical reactions?
searchText: chapter 2.4: how do atoms rearrange in different types of chemical reactions?

title: 2.4 Concept 1: A compound forms in a synthesis reaction and breaks down in a decomposition reaction — p.156–159
searchText: 2.4 concept 1: a compound forms in a synthesis reaction and breaks down in a decomposition reaction — p.156–159

title: KP1: Synthesis reaction
searchText: kp1: synthesis reaction a synthesis reaction is a chemical reaction in which two or more reactants combine to produce a single product. identify synthesis reactions by looking for several reactants forming one compound.

title: KP2: General synthesis pattern
searchText: kp2: general synthesis pattern general pattern: \(a + b ab\). use the pattern to classify reactions such as metal + non-metal forming an ionic compound.

title: KP3: Synthesis and energy release
searchText: kp3: synthesis and energy release many synthesis reactions are exothermic because bond formation releases energy overall. connect heat, light, or other energy release to product formation.

title: KP4: Synthesis in the environment and technology
searchText: kp4: synthesis in the environment and technology synthesis reactions occur in processes such as acid precipitation, rust formation, and hydrogen fuel cells. recognize synthesis reactions in environmental and technological contexts.

title: KP5: Decomposition reaction
searchText: kp5: decomposition reaction a decomposition reaction is a chemical reaction in which one compound breaks down into elements or simpler compounds. identify decomposition reactions by looking for one reactant forming two or more products.

title: KP6: General decomposition pattern
searchText: kp6: general decomposition pattern general pattern: \(ab a + b\). treat decomposition as the reverse pattern of synthesis.

title: KP7: Decomposition and energy input
searchText: kp7: decomposition and energy input most decomposition reactions are endothermic because the reactant must absorb enough energy for bonds to break. explain why heat or electricity may be needed to break a compound apart.
