title: 2.4 Concept 2: In replacement reactions, elements replace other elements — p.160–164
searchText: 2.4 concept 2: in replacement reactions, elements replace other elements — p.160–164

title: KP1: Single replacement reaction
searchText: kp1: single replacement reaction a single replacement reaction occurs when an element and a compound react to produce another element and another compound. identify reactions where one element takes the place of another element in a compound.

title: KP2: Metal replacement pattern
searchText: kp2: metal replacement pattern general pattern: \(a + bx ax + b\). recognize when a metal element replaces a metal ion in an aqueous compound.

title: KP3: Non-metal replacement pattern
searchText: kp3: non-metal replacement pattern general pattern: \(ax + y ay + x\). recognize when a non-metal element replaces a non-metal ion in a compound.

title: KP4: Hydrogen as a replaceable element
searchText: kp4: hydrogen as a replaceable element in some single replacement reactions, hydrogen in water can behave like a metal. explain how a metal reacting with water can produce hydrogen gas.

title: KP5: Applications of single replacement reactions
searchText: kp5: applications of single replacement reactions some single replacement reactions release large amounts of energy or produce useful elements. connect single replacement reactions to bromine production, welding railroad tracks, or joining electrical conductors.

title: KP6: Double replacement reaction
searchText: kp6: double replacement reaction a double replacement reaction occurs when the positive ions of two ionic compounds change places to form two new ionic compounds. identify reactions where ions exchange partners in aqueous solution.

title: KP7: Precipitate formation
searchText: kp7: precipitate formation a precipitate is a solid that forms from a reaction between aqueous solutions. use formation of a solid as evidence for some double replacement reactions.

title: 2.4 Concept 3: Most combustion reactions release heat and light — p.165–167
searchText: 2.4 concept 3: most combustion reactions release heat and light — p.165–167

title: KP1: Combustion reaction
searchText: kp1: combustion reaction a combustion reaction is a chemical reaction in which an element reacts with oxygen to produce an oxide and heat. identify combustion by the involvement of oxygen and energy release.

title: KP2: Hydrocarbon combustion
searchText: kp2: hydrocarbon combustion hydrocarbons contain carbon and hydrogen; complete combustion produces carbon dioxide and water. classify burning fuels such as methane, gasoline, and propane as combustion reactions.

title: KP3: General hydrocarbon combustion pattern
searchText: kp3: general hydrocarbon combustion pattern general pattern: \(c xh y + o 2 co 2 + h 2o\). predict the common products of complete hydrocarbon combustion.

title: KP4: Combustion as an exothermic process
searchText: kp4: combustion as an exothermic process most combustion reactions release heat and light. use flame, heat, and light as evidence of energy release.

title: KP5: Combustion and pollution
searchText: kp5: combustion and pollution combustion of sulfur or nitrogen-containing substances can produce pollutants such as sulfur dioxide or nitrogen dioxide. connect combustion products to air pollution and later acid-forming reactions.

title: KP6: Incomplete combustion
searchText: kp6: incomplete combustion incomplete combustion occurs when oxygen supply is too low and can produce carbon monoxide and soot. explain why burning fuels indoors or in poorly ventilated spaces can be dangerous.

title: KP7: Carbon monoxide hazard
searchText: kp7: carbon monoxide hazard carbon monoxide is a colourless, odourless, highly toxic gas. connect incomplete combustion to the need for carbon monoxide detectors.

title: 2.4 Concept 4: In a neutralization reaction, an acid reacts with a base — p.168–174
searchText: 2.4 concept 4: in a neutralization reaction, an acid reacts with a base — p.168–174

title: KP1: Acid
searchText: kp1: acid an acid is a compound that forms \(h +\) ions when dissolved in water. identify acidic solutions from formulas, properties, or ion formation.

title: KP2: Base
searchText: kp2: base a base is a compound that forms \(oh -\) ions when dissolved in water. identify basic solutions from formulas, properties, or ion formation.

title: KP3: Acid-base indicators
searchText: kp3: acid-base indicators an acid-base indicator changes colour in response to whether a solution is acidic or basic. use litmus or other indicators to classify unknown solutions.

title: KP4: pH scale
searchText: kp4: ph scale the ph scale ranges from 0 to 14 and measures acidity or basicity. classify solutions as acidic \((ph 7)\).

title: KP5: pH and ion concentration
searchText: kp5: ph and ion concentration acids have more \(h +\) than \(oh -\); bases have more \(oh -\) than \(h +\); neutral solutions have equal amounts. connect ph values to relative hydrogen-ion and hydroxide-ion concentrations.

title: KP6: Neutralization reaction
searchText: kp6: neutralization reaction a neutralization reaction occurs when an acid reacts with a base to form water and a salt. recognize neutralization as a common acid-base reaction in labs, medicine, and the environment.

title: KP7: General neutralization pattern
searchText: kp7: general neutralization pattern general pattern: \(ha + boh ba + hoh\), or acid + base \( \) salt + water. explain why neutralization is a type of double replacement reaction.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: What are the consequences of increasing ocean acidity? — p.176–177
searchText: optional extension — at issue: what are the consequences of increasing ocean acidity? — p.176–177

title: KP1: Fossil fuels and carbon dioxide
searchText: kp1: fossil fuels and carbon dioxide burning fossil fuels releases carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. connect combustion to increasing atmospheric \(co 2\).

title: KP2: Carbon dioxide and ocean acidity
searchText: kp2: carbon dioxide and ocean acidity \(co 2\) dissolves in ocean water and can form carbonic acid. explain how atmospheric \(co 2\) affects ocean chemistry.

title: KP3: Ocean organisms and shells
searchText: kp3: ocean organisms and shells increased acidity can affect organisms that build shells or skeletons from carbonate materials. connect ocean acidification to food webs and human communities that depend on ocean organisms.

title: KP4: Chemistry and global consequences
searchText: kp4: chemistry and global consequences chemical changes in the ocean can affect ecosystems, food supply, and livelihoods. evaluate ocean acidity as both a chemistry issue and a social-environmental issue.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-G: Using Acid and Base Properties to Identify Four Unknowns — p.178–179
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 2-g: using acid and base properties to identify four unknowns — p.178–179

title: KP1: Properties of acids and bases
searchText: kp1: properties of acids and bases acids and bases can be identified using observable properties and tests. use evidence such as indicator colour, ph, or reaction behaviour to classify unknowns.

title: KP2: Testing unknown solutions
searchText: kp2: testing unknown solutions unknown substances can be identified by comparing test results with known acid-base properties. design a safe procedure for identifying unknown acidic, basic, or neutral substances.

title: KP3: Evidence-based identification
searchText: kp3: evidence-based identification reliable identification requires organized observations and comparison across multiple tests. support a claim about an unknown substance using recorded evidence.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-H: Six Types of Reactions — p.180–182
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 2-h: six types of reactions — p.180–182

title: KP1: Reaction classification
searchText: kp1: reaction classification chemical reactions can be classified as synthesis, decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, combustion, or neutralization. sort reactions by reactant-product patterns.

title: KP2: Predicting products
searchText: kp2: predicting products reaction type can help predict likely products. use patterns to predict products before balancing the equation.

title: KP3: Balanced equations
searchText: kp3: balanced equations reaction classification and product prediction must still follow conservation of mass. balance predicted chemical equations using coefficients.

title: KP4: Observation and reaction type
searchText: kp4: observation and reaction type visible evidence such as gas formation, precipitate formation, heat, light, or colour change can support reaction classification. connect lab observations to reaction-type reasoning.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-I: Evaluate the Effectiveness of Antacids — p.183
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 2-i: evaluate the effectiveness of antacids — p.183

title: KP1: Antacids and neutralization
searchText: kp1: antacids and neutralization antacids work by neutralizing stomach acid. connect acid-base reactions to medicine and digestion.

title: KP2: Experimental variables
searchText: kp2: experimental variables a fair antacid test needs one independent variable, one dependent variable, and controlled conditions. design an experiment that compares antacids using consistent criteria.

title: KP3: Effectiveness criteria
searchText: kp3: effectiveness criteria antacid effectiveness may depend on amount needed, cost, side effects, and neutralizing ability. choose an antacid recommendation based on evidence rather than one measurement alone.

title: KP4: Balanced neutralization equations
searchText: kp4: balanced neutralization equations each antacid reaction can be represented with a balanced neutralization equation. link experimental results to the chemical reaction that neutralizes acid.

title: Unit 3: Energy is conserved and its transformation affects living things and the environment
searchText: unit 3: energy is conserved and its transformation affects living things and the environment

title: Chapter 3.1: What are the properties of energy?
searchText: chapter 3.1: what are the properties of energy?

title: 3.1 Concept 1: Energy can produce change in a system — p.200–201
searchText: 3.1 concept 1: energy can produce change in a system — p.200–201

title: KP1: Energy as an indirect concept
searchText: kp1: energy as an indirect concept energy cannot be observed directly, but its effects can be observed. identify energy by the changes it causes in objects, motion, temperature, sound, or position.

title: KP2: Energy and change
searchText: kp2: energy and change energy can cause change in a system. use observed change as evidence that energy is present or being used.

title: KP3: System
searchText: kp3: system a system is anything under observation. define the object, process, or group being studied before analyzing energy.

title: KP4: Surroundings
searchText: kp4: surroundings the surroundings include everything outside the system. separate the system from the environment when tracking energy changes.

title: KP5: Properties of energy
searchText: kp5: properties of energy energy can take different forms, be transferred or transformed, and depend on different physical quantities. use these properties to organize energy observations in real situations.

title: 3.1 Concept 2: There are different forms of energy — p.202–205
searchText: 3.1 concept 2: there are different forms of energy — p.202–205

title: KP1: Kinetic energy
searchText: kp1: kinetic energy kinetic energy is the energy of motion. identify moving objects, moving particles, moving electrons, and vibrations as examples of kinetic energy.

title: KP2: Potential energy
searchText: kp2: potential energy potential energy is stored energy due to an object’s condition or position. identify stored energy in height, chemical bonds, stretched objects, separated charges, magnets, or atomic nuclei.

title: KP3: Mechanical kinetic energy
searchText: kp3: mechanical kinetic energy mechanical kinetic energy is the energy of motion of objects larger than atoms and molecules. identify moving bicycles, balls, vehicles, or people as systems with mechanical kinetic energy.

title: KP4: Thermal energy and heat
searchText: kp4: thermal energy and heat thermal energy is the random motion of particles; heat is thermal energy transferred from one object to another. distinguish thermal energy from heat in scientific language.

title: KP5: Radiant, electrical kinetic, and sound energy
searchText: kp5: radiant, electrical kinetic, and sound energy radiant energy travels as electromagnetic waves; electrical kinetic energy is moving electrons; sound energy is vibration of matter. classify light, current, lightning, and sound waves as forms of energy.

title: KP6: Forms of potential energy
searchText: kp6: forms of potential energy gravitational, chemical, elastic, magnetic, electrical, and nuclear energy are forms of potential energy. match stored energy examples to the correct potential energy category.

title: KP7: Energy in multiple forms
searchText: kp7: energy in multiple forms a system can have both kinetic energy and potential energy at the same time. analyze moving objects above a reference point, such as a cyclist or roller coaster.

title: 3.1 Concept 3: Energy can be transferred or transformed — p.206–208
searchText: 3.1 concept 3: energy can be transferred or transformed — p.206–208

title: KP1: Law of conservation of energy
searchText: kp1: law of conservation of energy energy is neither created nor destroyed; it is transferred or transformed. track energy before and after an energy change.

title: KP2: Energy transformation
searchText: kp2: energy transformation energy transformation occurs when energy changes from one form to another. describe chemical potential energy changing into electrical, radiant, or thermal energy.

title: KP3: Energy transfer
searchText: kp3: energy transfer energy transfer occurs when energy moves from one object or place to another while staying in the same form. identify energy moving between objects, such as one pool ball transferring mechanical kinetic energy to another.

title: KP4: Useful and unusable energy
searchText: kp4: useful and unusable energy no energy transformation is 100% efficient; some energy becomes less useful for the intended task. explain why thermal energy and sound energy often appear as byproducts.

title: KP5: Energy and system types
searchText: kp5: energy and system types open systems exchange matter and energy; closed systems exchange energy but not matter; isolated systems exchange neither matter nor energy. classify systems by what can enter or leave them.

title: KP6: System-surroundings energy exchange
searchText: kp6: system-surroundings energy exchange when a system releases energy, the surroundings absorb it; when a system absorbs energy, the surroundings release it. track energy movement between a system and its surroundings.

title: KP7: Transfer and transformation together
searchText: kp7: transfer and transformation together real situations often involve both energy transfer and energy transformation. analyze a catapult, light bulb, or pool-ball collision by naming both movement and form changes of energy.

title: 3.1 Concept 4: Different variables contribute to different forms of energy — p.209–214
searchText: 3.1 concept 4: different variables contribute to different forms of energy — p.209–214

title: KP1: Physical quantities and energy
searchText: kp1: physical quantities and energy different physical quantities contribute to different forms of energy. identify which variables matter before comparing energy in two systems.

title: KP2: Mechanical kinetic energy variables
searchText: kp2: mechanical kinetic energy variables mechanical kinetic energy depends on mass and velocity. predict that greater mass or greater speed increases mechanical kinetic energy.

title: KP3: Mechanical kinetic energy equation
searchText: kp3: mechanical kinetic energy equation \(e k = 1 2 mv 2\), where \(e k\) is mechanical kinetic energy, \(m\) is mass, and \(v\) is velocity. explain why doubling velocity has a larger effect than doubling mass.

title: KP4: Gravitational potential energy variables
searchText: kp4: gravitational potential energy variables gravitational potential energy depends on mass, gravitational field strength, and change in height from a reference point. predict how height, mass, and gravity affect stored gravitational energy.

title: KP5: Gravitational potential energy equation
searchText: kp5: gravitational potential energy equation \(e g = mg h\), where \(e g\) is gravitational potential energy, \(m\) is mass, \(g\) is acceleration due to gravity, and \( h\) is change in height. relate gravitational potential energy to falling objects, hills, cliffs, and amusement rides.

title: KP6: Reference position
searchText: kp6: reference position gravitational potential energy is measured relative to a chosen reference position. explain why the same object can have different \(e g\) values depending on the reference point.

title: KP7: Friction and apparent energy loss
searchText: kp7: friction and apparent energy loss friction transforms mechanical energy into thermal energy and sound energy. explain why a sled does not return to the same height even though total energy is conserved.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-A: Build a Rube Goldberg Machine — p.216
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 3-a: build a rube goldberg machine — p.216

title: KP1: Chain of energy changes
searchText: kp1: chain of energy changes a rube goldberg machine uses a sequence of energy transfers and transformations to complete a task. identify how one energy change triggers the next part of a system.

title: KP2: System design
searchText: kp2: system design the design of a system affects how energy is transferred and transformed. improve a machine by reducing unwanted energy losses or making transfers more reliable.

title: KP3: Energy evidence
searchText: kp3: energy evidence motion, sound, height change, collision, and deformation can provide evidence of energy transfer or transformation. record visible and audible evidence to explain how the machine works.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-B: Investigating Energy Transformation and Transfer — p.217
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 3-b: investigating energy transformation and transfer — p.217

title: KP1: Observing energy transformation
searchText: kp1: observing energy transformation energy transformation can be investigated by observing how a system changes form or motion. describe how energy changes form during a hands-on system.

title: KP2: Observing energy transfer
searchText: kp2: observing energy transfer energy transfer can be investigated by observing how energy moves between parts of a system or to surroundings. track where energy moves during an investigation.

title: KP3: Variables affecting energy
searchText: kp3: variables affecting energy design variables can change how much energy is transferred or transformed. identify which design changes affect system performance.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-C: Roller Coaster Physics — p.218–219
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 3-c: roller coaster physics — p.218–219

title: KP1: Roller coaster energy changes
searchText: kp1: roller coaster energy changes roller coasters transform gravitational potential energy into mechanical kinetic energy and back again. analyze energy changes at the top, bottom, and middle of hills.

title: KP2: Track height and speed
searchText: kp2: track height and speed higher positions have more gravitational potential energy, while faster motion has more mechanical kinetic energy. predict where a coaster has maximum \(e g\) and maximum \(e k\).

title: KP3: Friction and realistic motion
searchText: kp3: friction and realistic motion friction transforms some mechanical energy into thermal and sound energy. explain why real roller coasters cannot keep reaching the same height without added energy.

title: Chapter 3.2: How is energy transformed?
searchText: chapter 3.2: how is energy transformed?

title: 3.2 Concept 1: Energy is transformed in chemical reactions — p.222–227
searchText: 3.2 concept 1: energy is transformed in chemical reactions — p.222–227

title: KP1: Chemical potential energy
searchText: kp1: chemical potential energy chemical reactions can transform energy stored in chemical bonds into other forms of energy. identify chemical potential energy as the stored energy source in fuels, food, and biological molecules.

title: KP2: System and surroundings in chemical reactions
searchText: kp2: system and surroundings in chemical reactions in a chemical reaction, the reacting materials are the system, and energy is released to or absorbed from the surroundings. track whether energy moves from the system to the surroundings or from the surroundings into the system.

title: KP3: Bond energy and reaction energy
searchText: kp3: bond energy and reaction energy the amount of energy transformed depends on the nature of the chemical bonds in the reactants and products. compare reactant and product chemical potential energy to predict energy release or absorption.

title: KP4: Exothermic and endothermic reactions
searchText: kp4: exothermic and endothermic reactions if reactants have higher chemical potential energy than products, the reaction is exothermic; if reactants have lower chemical potential energy than products, the reaction is endothermic. classify reactions by comparing energy stored before and after the reaction.

title: KP5: Cellular respiration
searchText: kp5: cellular respiration cellular respiration transforms chemical potential energy in glucose into atp and other useful energy forms: \(c 6h 12 o 6 + 6o 2 6co 2 + 6h 2o + energy \). explain how living things release energy from glucose in controlled steps instead of by burning.

title: KP6: Photosynthesis
searchText: kp6: photosynthesis photosynthesis transforms light energy into chemical potential energy stored in glucose: \(6co 2 + 6h 2o + energy c 6h 12 o 6 + 6o 2\). connect chlorophyll, light energy, atp, and glucose production in plants.

title: KP7: Fossil fuels and fuel cells
searchText: kp7: fossil fuels and fuel cells fossil fuels store chemical potential energy from ancient solar energy, while fuel cells transform chemical potential energy directly into electrical energy. compare combustion of fossil fuels with hydrogen fuel cells in terms of energy transformation and pollution.

title: 3.2 Concept 2: Energy is transformed in nuclear reactions — p.228–232
searchText: 3.2 concept 2: energy is transformed in nuclear reactions — p.228–232

title: KP1: Nuclear reactions
searchText: kp1: nuclear reactions nuclear reactions involve changes in atomic nuclei and transform much more energy than chemical reactions. distinguish chemical reactions, which rearrange atoms, from nuclear reactions, which change nuclei.

title: KP2: Isotopes and radioactive isotopes
searchText: kp2: isotopes and radioactive isotopes isotopes have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons; unstable isotopes are radioactive. explain why some nuclei emit particles or radiation to become more stable.

title: KP3: Nuclear decay and radiation
searchText: kp3: nuclear decay and radiation nuclear decay is a change to an atom due to emission of particles or radiation; radiation transfers energy as electromagnetic waves. connect radioactive decay to energy release from unstable nuclei.

title: KP4: Alpha, beta, and gamma decay
searchText: kp4: alpha, beta, and gamma decay alpha decay emits a helium nucleus; beta decay emits a fast-moving electron; gamma decay emits a high-energy photon. compare how each decay type changes, or does not change, the atomic number and mass number.

title: KP5: Nuclear fission
searchText: kp5: nuclear fission nuclear fission splits a heavy nucleus into smaller nuclei with the release of energy. describe how uranium-235 can split after absorbing a neutron.

title: KP6: Chain reactions and nuclear reactors
searchText: kp6: chain reactions and nuclear reactors a chain reaction occurs when neutrons from one fission reaction trigger more fission reactions. explain how reactors control fission energy and transform thermal energy into electrical energy.

title: KP7: Nuclear fusion
searchText: kp7: nuclear fusion nuclear fusion combines very small nuclei into a larger nucleus and releases energy. compare fusion in the sun with fission in reactors, including energy per unit mass and radioactive waste.

title: 3.2 Concept 3: Energy is transformed when light energy interacts with matter — p.234–235
searchText: 3.2 concept 3: energy is transformed when light energy interacts with matter — p.234–235

title: KP1: Absorption
searchText: kp1: absorption absorption occurs when energy is taken up by matter without being reflected or transmitted. identify absorption as the first step in many light-energy transformations.

title: KP2: Excited electrons
searchText: kp2: excited electrons when matter absorbs light energy, electrons can move to higher energy levels. explain how excited electrons allow light energy to become another form of energy.

title: KP3: Chlorophyll and high-energy bonds
searchText: kp3: chlorophyll and high-energy bonds chlorophyll absorbs light energy, and this energy can be used to form high-energy chemical bonds. connect light absorption to chemical potential energy storage in photosynthesis.

title: KP4: Photovoltaic cells
searchText: kp4: photovoltaic cells photovoltaic cells transform light energy directly into electrical kinetic energy. explain how absorbed light frees electrons and produces electric current.

title: KP5: Vision and rhodopsin
searchText: kp5: vision and rhodopsin rhodopsin absorbs light energy, causing molecular changes that begin an electrical signal to the brain. trace light energy transformation from retina cells to nerve signals.

title: KP6: Light-energy transformation in different systems
searchText: kp6: light-energy transformation in different systems the way absorbed light energy is used depends on the properties and location of the absorbing molecule or material. compare chlorophyll, photovoltaic cells, and human vision as examples of light-energy transformation.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Effects of Radiation on Living Tissues — p.233
searchText: optional extension — at issue: effects of radiation on living tissues — p.233

title: KP1: Ionizing radiation
searchText: kp1: ionizing radiation ionizing radiation can remove electrons from atoms and break chemical bonds. explain how radiation can damage molecules, cells, tissues, and organs.

title: KP2: Alpha radiation risk
searchText: kp2: alpha radiation risk alpha particles have high mass and charge, so they deposit energy near the surface they strike. distinguish low external skin risk from high internal risk if alpha emitters are inhaled or ingested.

title: KP3: Beta radiation risk
searchText: kp3: beta radiation risk beta particles can penetrate the surface of matter and damage living tissue. explain why beta radiation can cause serious skin burns and is more dangerous if inhaled or ingested.

title: KP4: Gamma radiation risk
searchText: kp4: gamma radiation risk gamma rays have no mass or charge and can pass deeply through dense matter before interacting. explain why gamma radiation can ionize atoms anywhere in the body.

title: Optional Extension — Science at Work: Focus on Physics — p.236
searchText: optional extension — science at work: focus on physics — p.236

title: KP1: Energy transformation careers
searchText: kp1: energy transformation careers physics-related careers often require understanding how energy changes form or moves between systems. connect energy transformation to work in audiology, radiation protection, biomedical engineering, oceanography, materials design, meteorology, and archaeology.

title: KP2: Energy tools and technologies
searchText: kp2: energy tools and technologies technologies such as radar, magnetometers, radiation protection systems, and weather instruments depend on energy transfer or transformation. identify how physics concepts support real-world investigation, safety, design, and prediction.

title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take Action — Transforming Solar Energy to Make a Difference in Remote Areas — p.237
searchText: optional extension — make a difference: take action — transforming solar energy to make a difference in remote areas — p.237

title: KP1: Solar energy access
searchText: kp1: solar energy access solar-energy technologies can help provide electricity, clean water, and emergency supplies in remote or underserved areas. connect energy transformation to practical needs in rural communities and disaster relief.

title: KP2: Solar-powered community technologies
searchText: kp2: solar-powered community technologies solar devices can transform sunlight into electrical energy or use sunlight and gravity to support water filtration. compare the energy hub and sunsaluter as technologies that use solar energy for community benefit.

title: KP3: Solarships and sustainable delivery
searchText: kp3: solarships and sustainable delivery solarships combine buoyant lift, solar panels, and compact aircraft design to deliver supplies to remote areas. evaluate how solar energy can reduce reliance on fossil fuels in transportation.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Storing Energy in Salt — p.238
searchText: optional extension — at issue: storing energy in salt — p.238

title: KP1: Solar energy storage problem
searchText: kp1: solar energy storage problem solar-to-electrical energy transformation is reduced at night and on cloudy days. explain why large-scale solar power needs an energy-storage method.

title: KP2: Molten salt as thermal storage
searchText: kp2: molten salt as thermal storage mirrors can focus sunlight onto a tower where molten salt absorbs solar energy and stores it as thermal energy. trace energy transformation from sunlight to heated salt.

title: KP3: Stored thermal energy to electricity
searchText: kp3: stored thermal energy to electricity heated molten salt can later boil water into steam that drives a turbine to generate electrical energy. explain how stored thermal energy can help a solar power plant operate when sunlight is unavailable.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-D: Solar Cooker Challenge — p.240
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 3-d: solar cooker challenge — p.240

title: KP1: Solar cooker design
searchText: kp1: solar cooker design a solar cooker uses reflected and absorbed solar energy to heat water or food. design a device that concentrates sunlight safely and effectively.

title: KP2: Energy transfer and transformation
searchText: kp2: energy transfer and transformation solar energy can be reflected, absorbed, transformed into thermal energy, and transferred to water. explain how cooker shape, material, orientation, and container design affect heating.

title: KP3: Design testing and improvement
searchText: kp3: design testing and improvement engineering design improves through testing, comparing results, and modifying variables. use boiling time or temperature change as evidence for improving a solar cooker.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-E: Fission versus Fusion — p.241
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 3-e: fission versus fusion — p.241

title: KP1: Comparing nuclear processes
searchText: kp1: comparing nuclear processes fission splits heavy nuclei, while fusion combines light nuclei. create a clear comparison of reaction type, energy output, conditions, products, and waste.

title: KP2: Nuclear energy decisions
searchText: kp2: nuclear energy decisions fission is currently used for electrical energy generation because it can be controlled, while fusion remains difficult to control. explain why current nuclear power relies on fission rather than fusion.

title: KP3: Public communication about nuclear energy
searchText: kp3: public communication about nuclear energy scientific explanations of fission and fusion should be accurate, balanced, and appropriate for the audience. prepare educational material that compares benefits, risks, limitations, and future possibilities.

title: Chapter 3.3: How does energy transformation affect global systems?
searchText: chapter 3.3: how does energy transformation affect global systems?

title: 3.3 Concept 1: Earth is a system in which energy is transformed — p.244–245
searchText: 3.3 concept 1: earth is a system in which energy is transformed — p.244–245

title: KP1: Earth as a system
searchText: kp1: earth as a system earth can be considered a system because its processes can be observed and analyzed together. define earth, or part of earth, as the system before tracking energy transformations.

title: KP2: Energy transformations in Earth processes
searchText: kp2: energy transformations in earth processes earth’s wind, weather, tides, currents, volcanic activity, mountain building, and life processes depend on energy transformation. connect visible earth processes to energy changing form.

title: KP3: Solar energy in Earth’s system
searchText: kp3: solar energy in earth’s system solar energy drives movement of water in the atmosphere, hydrosphere, and over land, and sustains most life on earth. identify solar energy as the largest energy input affecting earth’s surface systems.

title: KP4: Gravitational potential energy in Earth’s system
searchText: kp4: gravitational potential energy in earth’s system gravitational potential energy helps move water, including through tides caused by the moon’s gravitational influence. connect tides and water movement to gravitational energy transformations.

title: KP5: Nuclear energy in Earth’s system
searchText: kp5: nuclear energy in earth’s system nuclear energy from radioactive decay in earth’s crust and mantle transforms into thermal energy. connect radioactive decay to volcanic activity, tectonic plate movement, geysers, and hot springs.

title: KP6: Relative energy impact
searchText: kp6: relative energy impact solar energy transformation has the greatest impact on earth’s system compared with gravitational and nuclear energy sources. prioritize solar energy when explaining large-scale atmospheric, hydrosphere, and life-supporting processes.

title: 3.3 Concept 2: Earth’s atmosphere is heated by the transformation and transfer of solar energy and thermal energy — p.246–249
searchText: 3.3 concept 2: earth’s atmosphere is heated by the transformation and transfer of solar energy and thermal energy — p.246–249

title: KP1: Solar radiation
searchText: kp1: solar radiation solar energy reaches earth as electromagnetic radiation, including visible light, infrared radiation, and ultraviolet radiation. identify incoming solar energy as radiation that can interact with air, water, land, and living things.

title: KP2: Absorption and reflection
searchText: kp2: absorption and reflection some incoming solar energy is absorbed by earth’s surface, while some is reflected back toward space. use absorption and reflection to explain why earth does not absorb all incoming solar energy.

title: KP3: Radiation from Earth’s surface
searchText: kp3: radiation from earth’s surface absorbed solar energy can be transformed into thermal energy and radiated again as infrared radiation. trace energy from incoming sunlight to thermal energy leaving earth’s surface.

title: KP4: Greenhouse gases
searchText: kp4: greenhouse gases greenhouse gases such as carbon dioxide, water vapour, nitrous oxide, and methane absorb infrared radiation. explain how greenhouse gases help warm earth’s atmosphere.

title: KP5: Greenhouse effect
searchText: kp5: greenhouse effect the greenhouse effect helps keep earth’s average temperature moderate enough for life. explain why earth would be much colder without naturally occurring greenhouse gases.

title: KP6: Conduction
searchText: kp6: conduction conduction is the transfer of thermal energy between substances that are touching. explain how warm land or water transfers thermal energy to nearby air molecules.

title: KP7: Convection
searchText: kp7: convection convection is the transfer of thermal energy by the movement of heated fluids, including gases and liquids. explain how warm, less-dense air rises and cooler, denser air sinks in the atmosphere.

title: 3.3 Concept 3: Energy transfer and energy transformation moderate Earth’s temperature — p.250–251
searchText: 3.3 concept 3: energy transfer and energy transformation moderate earth’s temperature — p.250–251

title: KP1: Water cycle and energy movement
searchText: kp1: water cycle and energy movement the water cycle moves water and energy among the hydrosphere, geosphere, and atmosphere. track both water and energy through evaporation, transpiration, condensation, and precipitation.

title: KP2: Evaporation
searchText: kp2: evaporation during evaporation, solar energy is transformed into thermal energy as water molecules move faster and enter the atmosphere as vapour. explain how evaporation moves thermal energy away from earth’s surface.

title: KP3: Transpiration
searchText: kp3: transpiration transpiration releases water vapour from plants into the atmosphere and moves thermal energy with it. connect plant processes to energy movement in the atmosphere.

title: KP4: Condensation
searchText: kp4: condensation as water vapour rises, cools, and condenses, thermal energy is released to the atmosphere. explain how cloud formation transfers energy to air molecules.

title: KP5: Precipitation and gravitational energy
searchText: kp5: precipitation and gravitational energy during precipitation, gravitational potential energy transforms into mechanical kinetic energy as water falls and flows downhill. connect rain, snow, runoff, and flowing water to energy transformation.

title: KP6: Specific heat capacity
searchText: kp6: specific heat capacity specific heat capacity is the amount of energy needed to change the temperature of \(1\,g\) of a substance by \(1 c\). use specific heat capacity to compare how quickly different materials warm or cool.

title: KP7: Oceans and temperature moderation
searchText: kp7: oceans and temperature moderation water has a high specific heat capacity, so oceans can store large amounts of thermal energy with small temperature changes. explain why coastal regions usually have cooler summers and warmer winters than inland regions.

title: 3.3 Concept 4: Energy transformation and transfer can harm aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems — p.252–253
searchText: 3.3 concept 4: energy transformation and transfer can harm aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems — p.252–253

title: KP1: Harmful energy transformations
searchText: kp1: harmful energy transformations energy transformations can harm ecosystems through natural events or human activities. compare natural sources such as forest fires or volcanoes with human-caused impacts such as climate change or radiation exposure.

title: KP2: Human-induced greenhouse effect
searchText: kp2: human-induced greenhouse effect human activities add greenhouse gases to the atmosphere, increasing earth’s average temperature. connect electrical generation, vehicles, heating homes, and industry to increased greenhouse gas emissions.

title: KP3: Aquatic ecosystems and temperature
searchText: kp3: aquatic ecosystems and temperature aquatic organisms often thrive within specific temperature ranges, so warming water can disrupt ecosystems. explain how species movement to cooler waters can affect food webs.

title: KP4: Coral bleaching
searchText: kp4: coral bleaching warming oceans can cause corals to expel algae that provide food, leading to coral bleaching and death. connect ocean temperature change to damage in reef habitats.

title: KP5: Food-web disruption
searchText: kp5: food-web disruption when prey species move because of warming waters, predators may lose reliable food sources. use the tufted puffin example to explain how warming ocean water can reduce survival of young animals.

title: KP6: Radiation exposure in terrestrial ecosystems
searchText: kp6: radiation exposure in terrestrial ecosystems terrestrial ecosystems can be strongly harmed by radioactive materials because these materials can accumulate in soil. explain why radiation exposure may persist longer on land than in aquatic systems.

title: KP7: Nuclear accidents and ecosystem damage
searchText: kp7: nuclear accidents and ecosystem damage radiation from nuclear accidents can damage organisms, cause mutations, and disrupt decomposition and nutrient cycling. connect fukushima and chernobyl examples to long-term ecosystem effects.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Storing Nuclear Waste Safely — p.254
searchText: optional extension — at issue: storing nuclear waste safely — p.254

title: KP1: Nuclear waste and radioactive decay
searchText: kp1: nuclear waste and radioactive decay nuclear wastes contain radioactive isotopes that release harmful radiation through nuclear decay over time. explain why some nuclear waste remains hazardous for a long time.

title: KP2: Nuclear waste classification
searchText: kp2: nuclear waste classification nuclear waste is classified by how much containment and isolation it requires. distinguish low-level, intermediate-level, and high-level waste by storage needs.

title: KP3: High-level waste storage
searchText: kp3: high-level waste storage used nuclear fuel gives off radiation and thermal energy, so it is cooled under water before being stored in concrete containers. explain why nuclear waste storage must manage both radiation and heat.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: The Not So Silent Sea — p.255
searchText: optional extension — at issue: the not so silent sea — p.255

title: KP1: Underwater noise pollution
searchText: kp1: underwater noise pollution marine vessels can produce underwater noise pollution in busy shipping routes. connect tankers and freighters in the salish sea to increased underwater sound energy.

title: KP2: Effects on orcas
searchText: kp2: effects on orcas noise pollution can interfere with orcas’ communication, food-finding, and stress levels. explain why sound energy can become an ecosystem problem.

title: KP3: Reducing noise impacts
searchText: kp3: reducing noise impacts engine and propeller noise can be reduced through design and regulation. evaluate whether noise pollution should be managed to protect endangered marine animals.

title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Be Inspired — Marilyn Slett: Advocating for an Indigenous Marine Response Centre — p.256
searchText: optional extension — make a difference: be inspired — marilyn slett: advocating for an indigenous marine response centre — p.256

title: KP1: Oil-spill response and marine ecosystems
searchText: kp1: oil-spill response and marine ecosystems diesel fuel and other marine contaminants can harm coastal and marine ecosystems. connect oil-spill response planning to ecosystem protection.

title: KP2: First Peoples as first responders
searchText: kp2: first peoples as first responders first peoples are often first responders to marine emergencies in b.c. coastal territories. explain why local and indigenous knowledge matters in emergency response.

title: KP3: Indigenous Marine Response Centre
searchText: kp3: indigenous marine response centre an indigenous marine response centre would combine western science and first peoples knowledge for faster and more effective spill response. evaluate how community capacity and local response time can reduce harm from marine emergencies.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-F: Specific Heat Capacity — p.258–260
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 3-f: specific heat capacity — p.258–260

title: KP1: Comparing water and soil
searchText: kp1: comparing water and soil water and soil absorb radiant energy differently and change temperature at different rates. use temperature data to compare the specific heat capacity of water and soil.

title: KP2: Air temperature above surfaces
searchText: kp2: air temperature above surfaces the temperature above water and soil changes as energy is absorbed, transferred, and released. explain how land and water surfaces affect local air temperature.

title: KP3: Specific heat capacity equation
searchText: kp3: specific heat capacity equation \(q = mc t\), where \(q\) is heat absorbed or released, \(m\) is mass, \(c\) is specific heat capacity, and \( t\) is temperature change. calculate heat transfer or specific heat capacity using measured data.

title: KP4: Sea breezes and land breezes
searchText: kp4: sea breezes and land breezes differences in heating and cooling between land and water can create local winds. explain daytime sea breezes and nighttime land breezes using specific heat capacity.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-G: Modelling Changes in Earth’s Systems — p.261–263
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 3-g: modelling changes in earth’s systems — p.261–263

title: KP1: Physical models of Earth systems
searchText: kp1: physical models of earth systems physical models can represent parts of complex earth systems. build and evaluate a model of the natural greenhouse effect.

title: KP2: Natural and human-induced greenhouse effects
searchText: kp2: natural and human-induced greenhouse effects a greenhouse-effect model should allow light in, absorb energy, transform it to thermal energy, and limit infrared radiation leaving the system. adjust variables to model how human-induced changes can affect atmospheric temperature.

title: KP3: Ocean carbon dioxide absorption
searchText: kp3: ocean carbon dioxide absorption oceans absorb \(co 2\), which can react with water to form carbonic acid. connect atmospheric \(co 2\) absorption to increasing ocean acidity.

title: KP4: Ocean acidity and shells
searchText: kp4: ocean acidity and shells increased acidity can make it harder for organisms such as corals, clams, and sea urchins to build shells or skeletons. use eggshell models to test how acidity affects calcium-containing shells.

title: KP5: Model strengths and limitations
searchText: kp5: model strengths and limitations models can reveal patterns but may simplify real earth systems. evaluate which parts of a greenhouse or ocean-acidity model are accurate and which are incomplete.

title: Chapter 3.4: How does energy transformation affect humans?
searchText: chapter 3.4: how does energy transformation affect humans?

title: 3.4 Concept 1: Energy transformations provide benefits, but not without risk of harm — p.266–267
searchText: 3.4 concept 1: energy transformations provide benefits, but not without risk of harm — p.266–267

title: KP1: Human reliance on energy transformations
searchText: kp1: human reliance on energy transformations human societies use technologies and understandings to benefit from energy transformations. identify both benefits and risks when evaluating a human use of energy.

title: KP2: Biological energy transformations
searchText: kp2: biological energy transformations plants transform solar energy into chemical potential energy, and cellular respiration transforms that energy for life functions. connect human survival to photosynthesis and cellular respiration.

title: KP3: Fire and early human technology
searchText: kp3: fire and early human technology fire-starting transformed chemical potential energy in wood into thermal and radiant energy. explain how fire supported cooking, lighting, and survival in colder climates.

title: KP4: Large-scale combustion and air pollution
searchText: kp4: large-scale combustion and air pollution burning wood, coal, and fossil fuels transforms chemical potential energy but can create pollution and health problems. connect steam engines, gas engines, smog, and air pollution to combustion-related energy transformations.

title: KP5: Cement and batteries
searchText: kp5: cement and batteries cement production and batteries provide major benefits but can also create greenhouse gas emissions or toxic waste. evaluate why useful energy technologies can still produce environmental harm.

title: KP6: Hydroelectric dams
searchText: kp6: hydroelectric dams hydroelectric dams transform gravitational potential and mechanical kinetic energy into electrical energy. compare the benefit of electrical energy with harms such as land flooding, methane release, and displacement of people.

title: KP7: Nuclear reactors and fuel cells
searchText: kp7: nuclear reactors and fuel cells nuclear reactors and fuel cells can provide useful energy, but each has risks or hidden impacts. compare nuclear medical and electrical uses with radiation risk, and fuel cell benefits with hydrogen-production emissions.

title: 3.4 Concept 2: We take action and create technology to reduce the harmful impacts of energy transformation — p.268–269
searchText: 3.4 concept 2: we take action and create technology to reduce the harmful impacts of energy transformation — p.268–269

title: KP1: Personal and national action
searchText: kp1: personal and national action reducing harmful impacts of technology-related energy transformation requires individual and collective choices. identify actions such as reducing energy use and choosing sustainable technologies.

title: KP2: Trade-offs in action
searchText: kp2: trade-offs in action reducing harm may require commitment and sacrifices in comfort or convenience. evaluate energy decisions by considering both benefit and sacrifice.

title: KP3: Student and community initiatives
searchText: kp3: student and community initiatives local actions can reduce energy use and support sustainable energy-transformation technologies. connect cycling, recycling, and urban farming to lower pollution or reduced manufacturing impact.

title: KP4: Technology is not problem-free
searchText: kp4: technology is not problem-free emerging technologies can reduce harm, but no technology is a complete cure-all. evaluate electric vehicles by considering both tailpipe emissions and electricity generation or battery disposal.

title: KP5: Nuclear fusion technology
searchText: kp5: nuclear fusion technology controlled nuclear fusion could generate electrical energy, but it requires extremely high temperatures and containment. explain why tokamak reactors use magnetic fields to contain super-heated fusion reactions.

title: KP6: Methane cracking and hydrogen
searchText: kp6: methane cracking and hydrogen methane cracking can produce hydrogen with much lower carbon dioxide emissions than steam methane reforming. evaluate hydrogen fuel cells by considering both use-stage emissions and hydrogen production.

title: KP7: Lower-emission cement
searchText: kp7: lower-emission cement new cement technologies aim to reduce carbon dioxide emissions or absorb carbon dioxide as cement hardens. explain why cement production is an important target for reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

title: 3.4 Concept 3: We learn from our mistakes and each other to reduce harmful impacts of energy transformation — p.270–271
searchText: 3.4 concept 3: we learn from our mistakes and each other to reduce harmful impacts of energy transformation — p.270–271

title: KP1: Learning from past mistakes
searchText: kp1: learning from past mistakes societies can reduce harm by using new knowledge and changed perspectives to improve decisions. analyze past energy-related harms to identify safer future choices.

title: KP2: Fukushima Daiichi accident
searchText: kp2: fukushima daiichi accident the fukushima accident involved an earthquake, tsunami, power loss, disabled backup generators, reactor core meltdowns, explosions, and escaped radioactive materials. use the accident to explain how system failures can multiply during energy disasters.

title: KP3: Radiation exposure and cleanup
searchText: kp3: radiation exposure and cleanup nuclear accidents can expose people and environments to harmful radioactive materials. connect cleanup efforts and public safety planning to radiation risk.

title: KP4: Safety inspections and technology redesign
searchText: kp4: safety inspections and technology redesign after accidents, governments and engineers can inspect existing systems and improve technology to reduce future risk. explain how older reactor designs can be reassessed after a major failure.

title: KP5: Different national responses
searchText: kp5: different national responses countries may respond differently to the same accident, from reducing nuclear power use to developing safer nuclear technologies. compare energy policy decisions by safety, reliability, climate impact, and public trust.

title: KP6: Managing used nuclear fuel
searchText: kp6: managing used nuclear fuel used nuclear fuel must be managed and stored so that it does not harm life or land. explain why nuclear energy decisions must include long-term waste management.

title: KP7: Indigenous knowledge and Western science
searchText: kp7: indigenous knowledge and western science nuclear waste planning can include collaboration between indigenous knowledge and western science. evaluate why storage-site decisions should include local communities, first nations, inuit, and métis perspectives.

title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take a Stand — Should we use nuclear power? — p.272
searchText: optional extension — make a difference: take a stand — should we use nuclear power? — p.272

title: KP1: Nuclear power concerns
searchText: kp1: nuclear power concerns public opinion about nuclear power is shaped by accident risk and nuclear waste risk. identify major arguments against nuclear power.

title: KP2: Nuclear power benefits
searchText: kp2: nuclear power benefits nuclear power can be viewed as dependable, reduce dependence on energy imports, and contribute less to climate change than fossil fuels. identify major arguments in favour of nuclear power.

title: KP3: Fukushima and public trust
searchText: kp3: fukushima and public trust fukushima changed public perception by raising concern about accident management, slow information release, and institutional trust. explain why energy decisions depend on both technical evidence and public confidence.

title: KP4: Evidence-based position
searchText: kp4: evidence-based position a position on nuclear power should weigh safety, cost, waste, reliability, and climate change. prepare a reasoned argument for or against nuclear power.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 3-H: Energy Transformation Debate — p.274–275
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 3-h: energy transformation debate — p.274–275

title: KP1: Debate question
searchText: kp1: debate question the central question is whether technology-based energy transformations benefit society more than they harm it. choose an energy transformation and evaluate both benefits and harms.

title: KP2: Research planning
searchText: kp2: research planning a debate requires clear research questions, reliable resources, organized findings, and cited evidence. build an argument using evidence about energy transformations in transportation, electricity, agriculture, manufacturing, medicine, or food systems.

title: KP3: Bias and reliability
searchText: kp3: bias and reliability resources should be assessed for political position, special interests, funding, evidence, citations, balance, and emotional appeal. distinguish reliable evidence from biased or one-sided claims.

title: KP4: Evidence-based communication
searchText: kp4: evidence-based communication a strong energy debate uses facts, logic, and clear reasoning rather than unsupported opinions. present a balanced argument and evaluate which side is better supported.

title: Unit 4: The formation of the universe can be explained by the big bang theory
searchText: unit 4: the formation of the universe can be explained by the big bang theory

title: Chapter 4.1: What is the universe, and how do we make sense of it?
searchText: chapter 4.1: what is the universe, and how do we make sense of it?

title: 4.1 Concept 1: “Universe” is understood as different things to different people — p.294–296
searchText: 4.1 concept 1: “universe” is understood as different things to different people — p.294–296

title: KP1: Universe
searchText: kp1: universe the universe is all that exists everywhere, including matter, energy, planets, stars, galaxies, and the space containing them. use the term universe to describe the largest physical system being considered.

title: KP2: Universe as a story
searchText: kp2: universe as a story scientific explanations of the universe can be understood as stories built from observations, evidence, hypotheses, and theories. connect sky observations to scientific and cultural ways of explaining the universe.

title: KP3: Beginning of the universe
searchText: kp3: beginning of the universe a scientific story of the universe begins about 13.8 billion years ago. recognize the big bang time scale as far beyond ordinary human experience.

title: KP4: Scale of the universe
searchText: kp4: scale of the universe the size and age of the universe are difficult to imagine because they involve extremely large numbers. use scale thinking when describing distances, ages, galaxies, and cosmic events.

title: KP5: Changing scientific understanding
searchText: kp5: changing scientific understanding scientific understanding of the universe changes over time as evidence and interpretation improve. explain why the milky way was once thought to be the whole universe, but is now understood as one galaxy among billions.

title: KP6: Relative meaning of “all that exists”
searchText: kp6: relative meaning of “all that exists” the meaning of “all that exists” depends on what people know, where they live, and when they live. compare how different people may define the universe based on different experiences and knowledge.

title: KP7: Place and perspective
searchText: kp7: place and perspective a person’s sense of place can shape what they understand as their world or universe. use the ngorongoro crater example to explain how limited or expanded perspective can change understanding.

title: 4.1 Concept 2: The sense we make of the universe depends on who we are, when we are, and what we know — p.297
searchText: 4.1 concept 2: the sense we make of the universe depends on who we are, when we are, and what we know — p.297

title: KP1: Context shapes understanding
searchText: kp1: context shapes understanding a person’s understanding of the universe is shaped by identity, time period, place, knowledge, and experience. explain why people in different cultures or historical periods may interpret the universe differently.

title: KP2: Technology and worldview
searchText: kp2: technology and worldview technologies available in a time period affect how people communicate, observe, and understand the world. compare communication technologies from different eras to show how knowledge and daily life change.

title: KP3: Culture shock
searchText: kp3: culture shock culture shock is anxiety or disorientation caused by entering a dramatically new social or cultural situation. use culture shock to explain why people from different times or places may misunderstand each other’s knowledge and assumptions.

title: KP4: Time and place
searchText: kp4: time and place when and where a person lives affect what they think, believe, and understand. reflect on how historical period and location influence explanations of the universe.

title: KP5: Assumptions and bias
searchText: kp5: assumptions and bias ideas about the universe can be influenced by assumptions, bias, and limited perspective. question whether a view of the universe is based on evidence, tradition, culture, or personal experience.

title: KP6: Multiple ways of knowing
searchText: kp6: multiple ways of knowing scientific understanding can be considered alongside first peoples perspectives, local knowledge, and other ways of knowing. compare different knowledge systems respectfully when exploring the universe and human place within it.

title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take Action — What should we do about light pollution? — p.298
searchText: optional extension — make a difference: take action — what should we do about light pollution? — p.298

title: KP1: Light pollution
searchText: kp1: light pollution light pollution is artificial night lighting that prevents many people from seeing stars and the milky way. connect urban lighting to reduced sky visibility.

title: KP2: Benefits of night lighting
searchText: kp2: benefits of night lighting night lighting can improve safety and allow people to work, shop, and move after dark. weigh the benefits of lighting against environmental and astronomical costs.

title: KP3: Effects on living things
searchText: kp3: effects on living things light pollution can disrupt sleeping, feeding, migration, and breeding cycles. explain how birds and sea turtles can be harmed by artificial light at night.

title: KP4: Evidence-based response
searchText: kp4: evidence-based response responses to light pollution should consider affected groups, proposed solutions, and the effectiveness of those solutions. build an argument about whether and how communities should reduce light pollution.

title: Chapter 4.2: What do we know about the universe based on what we can see only with our eyes?
searchText: chapter 4.2: what do we know about the universe based on what we can see only with our eyes?

title: 4.2 Concept 1: We see a variety of objects that appear in the sky in mostly predictable ways — p.302–305
searchText: 4.2 concept 1: we see a variety of objects that appear in the sky in mostly predictable ways — p.302–305

title: KP1: Unaided-eye astronomy
searchText: kp1: unaided-eye astronomy before telescopes, people observed mostly the same sky objects that can still be seen with the unaided eye. distinguish observations possible with eyes alone from observations requiring technology.

title: KP2: The Sun
searchText: kp2: the sun the sun is the most obvious daytime sky object and appears to follow a predictable path across the sky. use the sun’s apparent motion to connect sky observations with time, seasons, and direction.

title: KP3: The Moon
searchText: kp3: the moon earth’s moon is the only moon visible to the unaided eye and changes appearance from night to night. use moon phases and movement patterns to explain how people observed time cycles.

title: KP4: Stars
searchText: kp4: stars on a clear, dark night, thousands of stars may be visible, differing in brightness, colour, location, and apparent motion. observe star patterns and motion to identify regular sky changes.

title: KP5: Visible planets
searchText: kp5: visible planets mercury, venus, mars, jupiter, and saturn can be seen without a telescope. distinguish planets from stars by noticing their wandering motion against background stars.

title: KP6: Other visible sky objects and events
searchText: kp6: other visible sky objects and events comets, meteors, novas, supernovas, and the milky way can also be visible without telescopes. classify visible sky objects as regular, temporary, or unusual events.

title: KP7: Place and sky observation
searchText: kp7: place and sky observation what people see in the sky depends on place, elevation, horizon, season, and local conditions. compare sky observations from different locations and explain why visible objects may differ.

title: 4.2 Concept 2: The appearance and motions of the objects we see can be described using a model called the celestial sphere — p.306–312
searchText: 4.2 concept 2: the appearance and motions of the objects we see can be described using a model called the celestial sphere — p.306–312

title: KP1: Constellations
searchText: kp1: constellations a constellation is a group of stars that forms a pattern in the sky. compare how different cultures may connect the same stars into different stories and shapes.

title: KP2: Asterisms and pointer stars
searchText: kp2: asterisms and pointer stars an asterism is a smaller star pattern within or across constellations; the big dipper is an asterism with pointer stars. use the big dipper’s pointer stars to locate polaris, the north star.

title: KP3: Polaris and navigation
searchText: kp3: polaris and navigation polaris is close to the north celestial pole and appears nearly fixed in the northern sky. use polaris to determine direction in the northern hemisphere.

title: KP4: Celestial sphere
searchText: kp4: celestial sphere the celestial sphere is an imaginary rotating sphere on which all visible sky objects appear to lie. use the celestial sphere model to describe apparent sky motion while remembering that earth rotates.

title: KP5: Celestial equator and celestial poles
searchText: kp5: celestial equator and celestial poles the celestial equator is earth’s equator projected into space; the celestial poles are where earth’s axis extends onto the celestial sphere. explain why some constellations visible from canada never set, while others never rise above the horizon.

title: KP6: Ecliptic and planetary motion
searchText: kp6: ecliptic and planetary motion the ecliptic is the sun’s apparent path across the celestial sphere, caused by earth’s orbit around the sun. connect the sun, moon, and planets to the same apparent sky path and explain seasonal visibility.

title: KP7: Retrograde motion
searchText: kp7: retrograde motion retrograde motion is the apparent backward motion of a planet against background stars, caused by earth passing an outer planet in its orbit. explain why mars can seem to slow, stop, reverse, and then move forward again.

title: KP8: Azimuth and altitude
searchText: kp8: azimuth and altitude azimuth measures direction along the horizon from north; altitude measures angular height above the horizon. describe a sky object’s position using observer-based coordinates.

title: KP9: Hand-angle estimates
searchText: kp9: hand-angle estimates at arm’s length, one finger is about \(1 \), one fist is about \(10 \), and an open hand span is about \(20 \). estimate the altitude and azimuth of the moon, planets, or stars from a specific location.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Ursa Major: How many stories over time and space? — p.314–315
searchText: optional extension — at issue: ursa major: how many stories over time and space? — p.314–315

title: KP1: Ursa Major across cultures
searchText: kp1: ursa major across cultures the same star pattern has been interpreted differently across cultures and historical periods. compare ursa major stories from greek, first peoples, african, arabic, chinese, and indian traditions.

title: KP2: Big Dipper and cultural meaning
searchText: kp2: big dipper and cultural meaning the big dipper can be understood as part of ursa major, a drinking gourd, a coffin, a celestial empire, or seven sages. explain how culture shapes meaning in sky patterns.

title: KP3: Sky stories and navigation
searchText: kp3: sky stories and navigation star stories can preserve practical knowledge about direction, seasons, and survival. connect the big dipper and polaris to navigation and cultural memory.

title: KP4: Astronomy and shared knowledge
searchText: kp4: astronomy and shared knowledge star names and sky traditions reflect contributions from many peoples, including astronomers of the islamic world. recognize astronomy as a shared human knowledge system shaped by many cultures.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 4-A: Using a Star Map — p.316–317
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 4-a: using a star map — p.316–317

title: KP1: Star maps
searchText: kp1: star maps a star map shows constellations and bright stars visible at a certain time of year and from a specific hemisphere or latitude range. use a northern hemisphere star map to identify constellations visible from canada.

title: KP2: Big Dipper as a guide
searchText: kp2: big dipper as a guide the big dipper’s pointer stars can help locate polaris and other constellations. use the big dipper to find the little dipper, cassiopeia, boötes, and leo.

title: KP3: Seasonal constellations
searchText: kp3: seasonal constellations different constellations are visible during different seasons. use a star map to identify constellations visible in each season.

title: KP4: Zodiac constellations
searchText: kp4: zodiac constellations zodiac constellations are visible during specific months and are along the path where planets can appear. locate zodiac constellations such as leo, virgo, scorpius, taurus, gemini, and cancer.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 4-B: Modelling Motion in the Night Sky — p.318–319
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 4-b: modelling motion in the night sky — p.318–319

title: KP1: Planisphere
searchText: kp1: planisphere a planisphere is a rotating star-map device used to predict visible constellations at a location and time. use date and time settings to identify which constellations are visible.

title: KP2: Apparent star motion
searchText: kp2: apparent star motion stars appear to move because earth rotates on its axis and revolves around the sun. explain why constellations appear to rotate around polaris.

title: KP3: Predictable sky patterns
searchText: kp3: predictable sky patterns careful observation allows scientists to model and predict the positions of celestial objects. use a model to compare visible constellations at different dates and times.

title: KP4: Model limitations
searchText: kp4: model limitations a planisphere simplifies the sky and is designed for particular locations and latitude ranges. evaluate when a planisphere is useful and when it may be inaccurate.

title: Chapter 4.3: How has technology expanded our knowledge and understanding of the universe?
searchText: chapter 4.3: how has technology expanded our knowledge and understanding of the universe?

title: 4.3 Concept 1: We have invented telescopes and other devices that extend and enhance our sense of sight — p.322–323
searchText: 4.3 concept 1: we have invented telescopes and other devices that extend and enhance our sense of sight — p.322–323

title: KP1: Limits of human vision
searchText: kp1: limits of human vision human eyes detect only visible light, so technology is needed to collect more light and detect other forms of radiation. explain why unaided-eye observations give only part of the information available from space.

title: KP2: Optical telescopes
searchText: kp2: optical telescopes optical telescopes collect visible light over a large area and concentrate it to form a brighter image. explain how galileo’s telescope allowed new observations such as jupiter’s moons and saturn’s rings.

title: KP3: Electromagnetic radiation from space
searchText: kp3: electromagnetic radiation from space visible light is only one form of electromagnetic radiation given off by stars and other objects in space. recognize that radio waves, infrared, ultraviolet, x-rays, and gamma rays can also carry information about space.

title: KP4: Radio telescopes
searchText: kp4: radio telescopes radio telescopes detect radio waves, including long-wavelength radio waves that can pass through clouds. explain why radio telescopes can observe space on cloudy days and at night.

title: KP5: Atmospheric absorption
searchText: kp5: atmospheric absorption earth’s atmosphere absorbs much of the radiation coming from space before it reaches the surface. explain why some telescopes must be placed above the atmosphere.

title: KP6: Space telescopes
searchText: kp6: space telescopes space telescopes observe radiation that is partly or fully blocked by earth’s atmosphere. connect chandra, spitzer, and hubble to observations using x-ray, infrared, visible, or ultraviolet radiation.

title: KP7: Multi-wavelength astronomy
searchText: kp7: multi-wavelength astronomy viewing the same object in different wavelengths reveals different physical features. use saturn images in visible, ultraviolet, infrared, and radio wavelengths to show how technology expands understanding.

title: 4.3 Concept 2: We know that our Milky Way galaxy is just one of many billions of galaxies in the universe — p.326–332
searchText: 4.3 concept 2: we know that our milky way galaxy is just one of many billions of galaxies in the universe — p.326–332

title: KP1: Galaxy
searchText: kp1: galaxy a galaxy is a collection of many billions of stars, plus gas and dust, held together by gravity. identify the milky way as the galaxy that contains the sun and all stars visible in the night sky.

title: KP2: Discovery of the Milky Way’s nature
searchText: kp2: discovery of the milky way’s nature telescopes helped show that the hazy milky way band is made of individual stars. connect galileo, william herschel, and caroline herschel to changing understanding of the milky way.

title: KP3: Galaxy formation and structure
searchText: kp3: galaxy formation and structure a galaxy forms when gravity causes a large, slowly spinning cloud of gas, dust, and stars to contract. explain why gravity is central to the formation and structure of galaxies.

title: KP4: Galaxy types
searchText: kp4: galaxy types galaxies can be classified as elliptical, spiral, barred spiral, or irregular based on shape. compare galaxy types using features such as central bulge, spiral arms, gas, dust, and young stars.

title: KP5: Observation angle and classification limits
searchText: kp5: observation angle and classification limits galaxy classification can be difficult because galaxies may be blocked by gas, dust, other objects, or viewing angle. explain why the same galaxy may appear different depending on how it is observed.

title: KP6: Star clusters and Milky Way mapping
searchText: kp6: star clusters and milky way mapping star clusters are groups of stars held together by gravity; globular cluster distribution helped show that the sun is not at the centre of the milky way. use harlow shapley’s reasoning to explain how astronomers inferred our position in the galaxy.

title: KP7: Local Group and larger structures
searchText: kp7: local group and larger structures the milky way belongs to the local group, which belongs to the virgo supercluster, one of millions of superclusters. place the milky way in a larger cosmic structure that includes andromeda and many other galaxies.

title: 4.3 Concept 3: There are vast distances separating stars and galaxies — p.334–337
searchText: 4.3 concept 3: there are vast distances separating stars and galaxies — p.334–337

title: KP1: Astronomical unit
searchText: kp1: astronomical unit one astronomical unit, or \(1\,au\), is the distance between earth and the sun, about \(150\,000\,000\,km\). use \(au\) for distances within the solar system.

title: KP2: Light-year
searchText: kp2: light-year a light-year is the distance light travels in one year, about \(9.46 10 12 \,km\). use light-years for distances between stars and galaxies.

title: KP3: Speed of light
searchText: kp3: speed of light light travels through space at about \(300\,000\,km/s\). connect the speed of light to the enormous scale of stellar and galactic distances.

title: KP4: Looking back in time
searchText: kp4: looking back in time the farther away an object is, the longer its light takes to reach earth. explain why observing vega means seeing it as it was about 25 years ago and why the sun appears about 8 minutes old.

title: KP5: Parallax
searchText: kp5: parallax parallax is the apparent change in position of an object against a fixed background when viewed from two different lines of sight. use parallax to explain how astronomers estimate distances to nearby stars.

title: KP6: Triangulation and parallax limits
searchText: kp6: triangulation and parallax limits astronomers use earth’s orbit as the known side of a triangle, but parallax becomes too imprecise for very distant stars. explain why satellites such as hipparcos and gaia improve distance measurements for many stars.

title: 4.3 Concept 4: The properties of stars help us develop an understanding of their life cycles — p.338–347
searchText: 4.3 concept 4: the properties of stars help us develop an understanding of their life cycles — p.338–347

title: KP1: Star properties
searchText: kp1: star properties important star properties include colour, temperature, luminosity, mass, and brightness. use observable properties to compare stars and infer their stage of development.

title: KP2: Colour and temperature
searchText: kp2: colour and temperature star colour is related to surface temperature; blue stars are hotter and red stars are cooler. infer relative temperature from star colour.

title: KP3: Luminosity and brightness
searchText: kp3: luminosity and brightness luminosity is a star’s actual energy output, while apparent brightness depends on both luminosity and distance from earth. avoid assuming the brightest-looking star is always the most luminous.

title: KP4: Stellar mass
searchText: kp4: stellar mass stellar mass is often compared with the sun’s mass, called one solar mass. use mass to predict how long a star remains active and what it may become.

title: KP5: Hertzsprung-Russell diagram
searchText: kp5: hertzsprung-russell diagram a hertzsprung-russell, or h-r, diagram compares star properties such as colour, temperature, luminosity, and absolute magnitude. use an h-r diagram to identify patterns among stars.

title: KP6: Main sequence stars
searchText: kp6: main sequence stars the main sequence is a diagonal band on the h-r diagram that contains about \(90\%\) of the stars visible from earth. explain why most stars are classified as main sequence stars.

title: KP7: Star life cycles and initial mass
searchText: kp7: star life cycles and initial mass a star’s life cycle depends mainly on its initial mass on the main sequence. compare low-mass, intermediate-mass, and high-mass star pathways.

title: KP8: Final stages of stars
searchText: kp8: final stages of stars stars can end as white dwarfs, neutron stars, or black holes, depending on their mass and evolution. connect red giants, supergiants, supernovas, neutron stars, and black holes to stellar life cycles.

title: KP9: Supernovas and heavy elements
searchText: kp9: supernovas and heavy elements high-mass stars can form heavier elements and eject them into space during supernovas. explain how elements in planets and living things can come from older stars.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Who has the MOST to lose? — p.324–325
searchText: optional extension — at issue: who has the most to lose? — p.324–325

title: KP1: MOST satellite
searchText: kp1: most satellite most, or microvariability and oscillation of stars, was canada’s first space telescope. connect most to the study of small changes in the brightness of sun-like stars.

title: KP2: Precision brightness measurements
searchText: kp2: precision brightness measurements most could detect very tiny changes in star brightness and monitor the same star for weeks. explain how long-term brightness data can reveal information about star life cycles.

title: KP3: Microsatellite design
searchText: kp3: microsatellite design microsatellites are smaller and less expensive than large space telescopes. compare most with hubble in terms of size, cost, and mission design.

title: KP4: Canadian space technology
