searchText: kp4: canadian space technology canadian researchers, engineers, agencies, and companies contributed to most’s design, launch, and continued use. evaluate how smaller space technologies can still make important scientific contributions.

title: Optional Extension — Science at Work: Focus on Astronomy — p.333
searchText: optional extension — science at work: focus on astronomy — p.333

title: KP1: Astronomy-related careers
searchText: kp1: astronomy-related careers astronomy connects with careers in science, engineering, data analysis, technology, and communication. identify how astronomy knowledge can support work beyond telescope observation.

title: KP2: Space weather and atmosphere
searchText: kp2: space weather and atmosphere some atmospheric scientists monitor space weather such as solar radiation storms. explain why space weather alerts matter for satellites, power grids, and radio communication.

title: KP3: Space technology systems
searchText: kp3: space technology systems space exploration depends on tools such as satellites, robotic arms, observatories, and communication systems. connect astronomy to engineering and technology used in space missions.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 4-C: Build an H-R Diagram — p.348
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 4-c: build an h-r diagram — p.348

title: KP1: H-R diagram construction
searchText: kp1: h-r diagram construction an h-r diagram can be built by graphing spectral type against absolute magnitude. use star data to identify the main sequence pattern.

title: KP2: Spectral types
searchText: kp2: spectral types main sequence stars can be grouped by spectral types \(o, b, a, f, g, k,\) and \(m\), with number subdivisions. use spectral type labels such as \(o5\), \(b2\), or \(g2\) to place stars on a graph.

title: KP3: Absolute magnitude prediction
searchText: kp3: absolute magnitude prediction a pattern on an h-r diagram can be used to predict the absolute magnitude of a main sequence star. estimate absolute magnitude for stars such as the sun, vega, or a \(b5\) star.

title: KP4: Graph-based reasoning
searchText: kp4: graph-based reasoning graphs can reveal relationships that are not obvious from a list of data. use an h-r diagram to support claims about star properties and life cycles.

title: Optional Extension — At Issue: How did Jocelyn Bell make one of the most important astronomy discoveries of the 20th century? — p.350–351
searchText: optional extension — at issue: how did jocelyn bell make one of the most important astronomy discoveries of the 20th century? — p.350–351

title: KP1: Neutron star hypothesis
searchText: kp1: neutron star hypothesis before neutron stars were observed, some astronomers proposed that supernovas could produce extremely dense neutron stars. connect theoretical prediction to later astronomical evidence.

title: KP2: Radio telescope data
searchText: kp2: radio telescope data jocelyn bell helped build a radio telescope and analyzed large amounts of chart-paper data. explain how careful data analysis can reveal unexpected patterns.

title: KP3: Pulsars
searchText: kp3: pulsars pulsars are pulsing radio sources later linked to neutron stars. connect repeated radio signals to the discovery of a new class of astronomical objects.

title: KP4: Recognition in science
searchText: kp4: recognition in science scientific discovery depends on observation, interpretation, collaboration, and fair recognition of contributions. evaluate why jocelyn bell’s role in the discovery of pulsars raises questions about fairness in science.

title: Chapter 4.4: How do we use the big bang theory to explain what we know about the universe?
searchText: chapter 4.4: how do we use the big bang theory to explain what we know about the universe?

title: 4.4 Concept 1: The big bang theory is based on two main sets of evidence: redshift and cosmic background radiation — p.354–358
searchText: 4.4 concept 1: the big bang theory is based on two main sets of evidence: redshift and cosmic background radiation — p.354–358

title: KP1: Big bang theory
searchText: kp1: big bang theory the big bang theory explains the origin of the universe, how it began, how it has changed, and how it is changing. use the theory to connect evidence about expansion with the history of the universe.

title: KP2: Cosmology
searchText: kp2: cosmology cosmology is the study of the origin, structure, development, and possible future of the universe. recognize the big bang theory as part of western scientific cosmology.

title: KP3: Doppler effect
searchText: kp3: doppler effect the doppler effect occurs when waves from a moving source are compressed or stretched. use changes in light wavelengths to infer motion of stars and galaxies.

title: KP4: Redshift
searchText: kp4: redshift redshift occurs when light from an object moving away is stretched toward longer, redder wavelengths. interpret redshifted galaxy spectra as evidence that galaxies are moving away from us.

title: KP5: Blueshift
searchText: kp5: blueshift blueshift occurs when light from an object moving toward an observer is shortened toward bluer wavelengths. interpret blueshifted spectra as evidence that an object is moving toward the observer.

title: KP6: Hubble’s law and expansion
searchText: kp6: hubble’s law and expansion hubble’s law shows that a galaxy’s speed away from earth is proportional to its distance from earth. use redshift-distance patterns as evidence that the universe is expanding.

title: KP7: Cosmic microwave background radiation
searchText: kp7: cosmic microwave background radiation cosmic microwave background, or cmb, radiation is leftover radiation from the big bang that fills the universe. use cmb radiation, including cobe and wmap observations, as evidence that the universe was once much hotter and denser.

title: 4.4 Concept 2: The big bang theory helps us describe how the components of the universe formed and have changed over time — p.360–361
searchText: 4.4 concept 2: the big bang theory helps us describe how the components of the universe formed and have changed over time — p.360–361

title: KP1: Looking back in time
searchText: kp1: looking back in time because light has a finite speed, looking farther into space means looking farther back in time. explain why distant galaxies show earlier stages of the universe.

title: KP2: Young universe and CMB
searchText: kp2: young universe and cmb the cmb shows the universe when it was about 380 000 years old and cool enough for photons to travel freely. connect cmb observations to an early stage of the universe.

title: KP3: Early expansion and inflation
searchText: kp3: early expansion and inflation soon after the big bang, the universe expanded extremely rapidly during a period called inflation. describe early expansion as a major step in the universe’s development.

title: KP4: Cooling and particle formation
searchText: kp4: cooling and particle formation as the universe expanded, it cooled, allowing energy, elementary particles, atoms, and later larger structures to form. use cooling to explain how the universe changed from energy-rich conditions to matter-filled conditions.

title: KP5: Formation of stars and galaxies
searchText: kp5: formation of stars and galaxies after the early universe cooled, the earliest stars and first galaxies began to form. place star and galaxy formation within the timeline after the big bang.

title: KP6: Solar system formation
searchText: kp6: solar system formation the sun and solar system formed much later than the earliest stars and galaxies. recognize that earth and the solar system are relatively late parts of cosmic history.

title: KP7: Technology and early-universe evidence
searchText: kp7: technology and early-universe evidence telescopes and particle accelerators help scientists study early galaxies and high-energy conditions similar to the early universe. connect the james webb space telescope and cern’s large hadron collider to investigations of the universe’s early history.

title: 4.4 Concept 3: There is much about the universe that we still cannot explain — p.362–365
searchText: 4.4 concept 3: there is much about the universe that we still cannot explain — p.362–365

title: KP1: Continuing mysteries
searchText: kp1: continuing mysteries even with modern technology, the universe still contains major mysteries about its structure, size, age, and evolution. recognize that scientific explanations are refined as new evidence is collected.

title: KP2: Dark matter
searchText: kp2: dark matter dark matter is matter inferred from gravitational effects but not directly seen because it does not emit light. explain why astronomers infer dark matter from galaxy rotation and galaxy-cluster observations.

title: KP3: Andromeda and missing mass
searchText: kp3: andromeda and missing mass stars in the andromeda galaxy move faster than predicted from visible matter alone. use unexpected star speeds as evidence for additional unseen mass.

title: KP4: Dark matter halos
searchText: kp4: dark matter halos dark matter is thought to form large halos around galaxies. explain why galaxy motion in groups and clusters suggests much more mass than visible matter shows.

title: KP5: Dark energy
searchText: kp5: dark energy dark energy is a name for the unknown cause of the accelerating expansion of the universe. use type ia supernova data to explain why scientists infer dark energy.

title: KP6: Accelerating expansion
searchText: kp6: accelerating expansion astronomers expected expansion to slow because of gravity, but evidence shows expansion began accelerating about 7 billion years ago. connect accelerating expansion to the need for a new explanatory idea.

title: KP7: Visible matter as a small fraction
searchText: kp7: visible matter as a small fraction current estimates suggest the universe is mostly dark energy and dark matter, while visible matter makes up only a small fraction. explain why the visible universe is not the same as everything that exists.

title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Be Inspired — Dr. Stephen Hawking (1942–2018) — p.359
searchText: optional extension — make a difference: be inspired — dr. stephen hawking (1942–2018) — p.359

title: KP1: Hawking and black holes
searchText: kp1: hawking and black holes stephen hawking contributed ideas about black holes, matter, time, and space. connect individual scientific work to changing ideas about the universe.

title: KP2: Science and perseverance
searchText: kp2: science and perseverance hawking continued scientific work after being diagnosed with als. recognize persistence and curiosity as important scientific attitudes.

title: KP3: Communicating complex ideas
searchText: kp3: communicating complex ideas hawking is remembered for both scientific insight and the ability to inspire public interest in the universe. connect scientific discovery with public communication and imagination.

title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 4-D: The Age of the Universe — p.366–367
searchText: optional investigation — investigation 4-d: the age of the universe — p.366–367

title: KP1: Redshift-distance relationship
searchText: kp1: redshift-distance relationship a galaxy’s redshift is related to its distance from earth. use redshifted spectral lines to estimate galaxy velocity.

title: KP2: Hubble constant
searchText: kp2: hubble constant the slope of a graph of galaxy velocity versus galaxy distance gives the hubble constant, \(h\). calculate \(h\) from a line of best fit.

title: KP3: Estimating the age of the universe
searchText: kp3: estimating the age of the universe the age of the universe can be estimated using \( 10 12 h \), where \(h\) is the hubble constant. use graph data to estimate cosmic age.

title: KP4: Megaparsecs and velocity
searchText: kp4: megaparsecs and velocity astronomers use megaparsecs for large distances and velocity to describe motion toward or away from earth. interpret units such as \( km/s/mpc \) when working with hubble’s law.

title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take a Stand — Terraforming: Are we developers or caretakers? — p.368
searchText: optional extension — make a difference: take a stand — terraforming: are we developers or caretakers? — p.368

title: KP1: Terraforming
searchText: kp1: terraforming terraforming is the proposed process of transforming an alien, lifeless world into a life-sustaining ecosystem. connect space science to questions about future colonization and planetary engineering.

title: KP2: Exoplanets and off-world possibilities
searchText: kp2: exoplanets and off-world possibilities exoplanets are planets outside our solar system, expanding ideas about possible future human habitats. compare solar-system colonization with broader possibilities beyond the solar system.

title: KP3: Ethical responsibility
searchText: kp3: ethical responsibility terraforming raises questions about whether humans should act as developers or caretakers of other worlds. evaluate terraforming using environmental responsibility, technological possibility, and alternatives for solving earth’s problems.
